More executions take place here than in any other province, while whispers of rebellion swirl.

entries.name: The White Fleet
entries: The city's docks are bright and perpetually busy with trade. It is home to the White Fleet, Castinella's navy, arguably one of the strongest in all of Etharis. The Inquisition is vigilant here, ensuring that no arcane threats enter. Though they pay lip service to the church, the sailors of the White Fleet are happy to be away from the Inquisition's prying eyes and accusing stares.

entries.name: Noble Houses
entries: The collapse of the House of Faro has left a power vacuum that is yet to be filled. Many minor noble houses scrabble for influence, but the Inquisition maintains a tight grip on all city activities. Whenever a house grows too powerful, it will invariably be brought low by the combined actions of smaller houses collaborating to prevent such an ascension, either through economic means or direct assassination if necessary.
When House Faro returns to its former glory, the Inquisition will know what true wrath looks like.

entries.name: Toletum
entries: Toletum's great city is frequently cloaked in noxious smoke from its constantly running forges. Most of the ore mined in the Corova Mountains is processed in the lower city, fabricating arms and weaponry in quantities that dwarf production elsewhere in Castinella.

The upper city is home to wealthy bureaucrats. Administrators and heads of businesses significantly influence legislative oversight of the peninsula. There is also pressure to expand the Inquisition far beyond the reaches of Castinella. The likelihood that this could instigate wars with other nations does not seem to bother these war enthusiasts and their well-built infrastructure for selling military supplies.

entries.name: Toletum Crime
entries: Organized crime is entrenched throughout the city, with a vibrant black market that resells stolen military armaments. Weapons, magical potions, spell scrolls, and arcane focuses cannot be procured legally, but they can be found if one knows where to ask the right questions.

Since arcane magic is illegal, many arcanists surviving in Castinella can make a good living using their talents for unlawful means. The Castibona family is one of these. The city's lawkeepers doggedly pursue them because many of Castibona's arcanists do jobs that oppose the Inquisition's war on magic. Their services are expensive, but they are best positioned to help someone escape the Inquisition's clutches.

In its campaign to root out the Castibonas, the Inquisition has contracted sympathetic crime syndicates that do not tolerate magic users. As such, fences and dealers exist who can procure illicit substances or dangerous weapons, and maybe even a magical potion or scroll. However, these dealers are quick to sell out individuals to the Inquisition if it becomes beneficial for them to do so.

entries.name: Therpena
entries: Therpena enforces the same harsh laws as the other provinces, but the general mood here is more joyful. This doesn't mean the Arcanist Inquisition is lax.

When the Inquisition came to power, they took Ember Cairn and the Shattered Peaks as their headquarters. The sunny hills now live within the shadow of the Inquisition and its much stricter rule. Local guardsmen are often less harsh and more forgiving than in other parts of the peninsula. In contrast, church enforcers are twice as diligent when the Inquisition's watchful eye is so close.

entries.name: Center of Art and Culture
entries: Therpena's noble houses have long been famous for the arts. This focus has led to freer and more joyful people, at least on the surface.

Today, most art celebrates the goodness of the Arch Seraphs and the fight against the evils of the arcane and Daemons. The church censures anything it deems heretical, and it burns books of magic gathered by the Inquisition. Many artistic works have been destroyed in the name of Empyreus.

Therpena's music and drama are without equal, attracting musicians from other lands to perform in the city. The competition for prestige is fierce. Audiences are discerning, and the skills that would win lucrative work in other kingdoms might garner only modest compensation here.

entries.name: The Calcien Family
entries: Thus far, the Inquisition has never thoroughly raided the Calcien family's many residences. Despite suggestions that the noble house has stockpiles of heretical art, cursory reviews have only uncovered appropriately reverent pieces.

The unspoken and publicly known secret is that the Calcien family generously supports the church, for which most of the family's activities are ignored. This includes the family's magical history from the time before the Inquisition. Accusations are said quietly, as many family members are part of the clergy, and their roots within the church run deep.

entries.name: Ember Cairn
entries: Destroyed long ago, the granite city of Ember Cairn stands atop the Shattered Peaks. It was rebuilt and repopulated by the once-nomadic dragonborn. It is the strictest and most regulated of Castinella's cities. Despite this, a black market still flourishes in the shadows.

There is a growing interest in uncovering the history of the dragonborn, dragons, and the secrets of the city's original destruction. The Inquisition carefully monitors this. They are concerned, as dragons were rumored to have been talented practitioners of arcane magic.

entries.name: The Cathedral of Embers
entries: Looming over Ember Cairn, the headquarters of the Arcanist Inquisition stands in the city's heart. As the church's seat of power, it is beautiful, solemn, and deadly for those who practice arcane magic or oppose the Castinellan government.

Many have heard of a labyrinth hidden beneath the Cathedral, where the Inquisition throws unfortunate arcanists they have captured. Some joke that food waste is dropped within, not to feed its prisoners but instead the many monsters rumored to lurk there. The extent of the Labyrinth is unknown. Some believe its passageways spread for miles as tales persist of miners intersecting its tunnels when they dig too deeply beneath the Shattered Peaks. Some seek out the Labyrinth, enticed by promises of lost treasures, untold wealth, and magical cures lost within.

entries.name: Hedric, the High Redeemer
entries: The leader of the Arcanist Inquisition's troops is an aging black dragonborn with a lower jaw misshapen by a scar. His hatred of arcane magic perfectly aligns with the goals and methods of the Inquisition. He sees danger everywhere, actively rooting out corruption with fanatical zeal, swiftly executing the High Inquisitor's directives.

entries.name: The Empyrean Pit
entries: Fighting pits where warriors fight hand-to-hand are growing in popularity. Ember Cairn's Empyrean Pit is the newest and most elaborate fighting arena in Castinella. It contains artwork from the most talented artisans of the day. Grand pit fighting spectacles are presented several times a month under the clergy's watchful eye. It's not unheard of for the Inquisition to quickly arrest combatants who accidentally reveal arcane powers in the heat of battle.

entries.name: The Valikan Clans
entries: In the far north, at the frozen edge of Etharis, spreads the home of the Valikan clans. It is a harsh land of short summers and frigid winters where the weak northern sun can fail to rise above the horizon.

The dangers of this land are both natural and supernatural. Its people have a hardened tenacity that is steeped in traditions of violence, not just to survive but also as a religious necessity to forestall the end of the world.

entries.name: Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes
entries: Ancient legends speak of a time when mighty {@book Primordials|GrimHollowPG24|9|The Primordials} rivaled the power of the gods. {@book Gormadraug|GrimHollowPG24|8|Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes}, the Great Prismatic Wyrm, was the mightiest, leaving destruction in his wake and coldfire erupting at every step.

Eventually, seven mortal heroes rose against the Primordials. The great {@book Kentigern|GrimHollowPG24|9|The Kentigern Saga} led this mythic band, defeating hordes of Elementals until finally facing Gormadraug himself. Despite being swallowed by the Great Wyrm, the heroes slayed it from within, and thus, the north was tamed. People soon arrived, forming six major clans, each taking the name of one of the legendary heroes who killed the Great Wyrm.
However, legends also state that Gormadraug was not truly defeated but is merely sleeping. It is prophesized that the Great Wyrm will one day rise again to consume the world.

entries.name: Raiding and Thralls
entries: Valika's brief growing season and limited resources have created a culture where raiding is seen as a necessity and an acceptable way of life. These raids inspire a deep fear across Etharis, where every spring, coastal settlements watch for longships on the horizon.

Valika is built on the labor of captured folk bonded to servitude. Raiding for thralls is an ancient clan tradition that most have no perspective to question. The north is a harsh land with few settlements, and the Valikans have never had enough people to farm, mine, fish, or hunt. A millennia ago, the clans looked southward for the folk they needed to keep their settlements and culture alive.

Captured thralls are returned to Valika and bonded for a fixed term of no more than five years. For most, it is a difficult but not insurmountable trial. A thrall's treatment varies from clan to clan, but generally, they are considered part of the community as they live, work, and eat alongside the clanfolk they serve. They enjoy the same freedom of movement as other clan members, but thralls captured alone outside their communities are easy fodder for the {@book Prismatic Circle's|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Prismatic Circle} sacrifices, wild beasts of the land, or the weather.

The Valikan clans also raid each other endlessly. Folk captured by another clan can be bonded into servitude as easily as those of foreign lands. However, such local raids effectively see the clans only trading members back and forth, so they focus more on stealing portable wealth, leaving thrall raids to the fleets reaving in the south.

entries.name: The Land
entries: This cold northern domain is immense, and its geography varies greatly. The inhospitable lands have forced those who live here to take extreme measures to survive.

entries.name: Grarjord
entries: Many southerners refer to the entirety of the north as Grarjord, but this ancient name only represents Valika's southernmost extent. It is where {@book Bürach's|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire} vast temperate woods give way to the open tundra, steep peaks, and cold forests surrounding the jagged fjord-cut peninsulas of the Teeth.

Grarjord is nominally under the control of {@book Thrull|GrimHollowPG24|8|Thrull}, with many minor clans living here to avoid the militaristic posturing of Clan Völgr. Resources remain scarce, and inter-clan relationships are often defined by raiding and blood feuds.

The beacon city of Halsfjord stands along the steep hills lining the fjord that shares its name. This is the seat of Clan Rune and where the clan's renowned skill with spellcraft is nurtured. The city is a repository of magical knowledge rivaling any arcane college in southern Etharis.

The sprawling port town of Skarfanes was a Valikan watchpost captured by the Bürach Empire some sixty years ago. The empire made the town an open trading hub, hoping to quell northern raids. Today, an imperial garrison protects the town, but local clan traders largely control it.

Given Thrull's perceived control of Grarjord, the Prismatic Circle's theocratic dictates hold sway, including the demands for isolationism and forbidding trade with outsiders, causing Skarvanes to be seen as a black market. However, these clans of Thrull circumvent the Circle's restrictions by secretly trading with smaller local clans less beholden to the Grand Druid's control.

entries.name: Thrull
entries: Thrull lies north of Grarjord, stretching from the island of Holgar to the foothills of the Grensfal Mountains. Much of this land lies north of the Circle of Shadow, an imaginary line marking the boundary of endless days and nights at the summer and winter solstices.

Much of Thrull is a vast tundra landscape, with seemingly endless stretches of rock, grass, and lichen, occasionally punctuated by great groves of oaks. These treed areas are known as Sentinel Woods, where the soil has turned blood-black from generations of murderous sacrifice by the {@book Prismatic Circle|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Prismatic Circle}. Nothing is known to lie north of Thrull, save the unforgiving Sea of Turmoil, where an ocean of restless ice hides leviathans in its frigid depths.

Tyburn, on the island of Holgar, is a bustling port and the seat of {@book Clan Völgr|GrimHollowPG24|8|Clan Völgr}, arguably the most dominant of Thrull's clans. Each raiding season, Völgr's fearsome longships depart from here to accost the south. Adding to its fearsome reputation is Hjadana, the Prsimatic Circle's Grand Druid. Her leadership influences nearly everything in Thrull, effectively making it a theocracy under her control.

Borgund, the City of Forges, is the most northern settlement on mainland Thrull and the seat of {@book Clan Sýr|GrimHollowPG24|8|Clan Sýr}. It is a massive complex built within the Armarka Mountains. Beneath stone-walled houses atop mountainside terraces is a vast network of chambers extending deep into the stony depths. Its great underground forges provide testament to Clan Sýr's legendary metalworking skills. Many of its citizens are thralls who elected to join Clan Sýr when their term of service ended, dedicating themselves to the artistry of metal.

Near central Thrull is the great frozen lake of Frostmere, which shares its name with an icy, frost giant fortress. These giants nurture a hatred of all people traveling across the nearby tundra, killing any they come into conflict with. A small minority of these giants ignore "the warm folk," and even fewer quietly trade with the clans.

entries.name: Hjadana, Grand Druid of the Prismatic Circle
entries: As the spiritual leader of Thrull, Hjadana uses her guile and the ferocious might of Clan Völgr to enforce a single vision of battle and sacrifice. The return of coldfire to the northlands has the Circle fearful that the Great Prismatic Wyrm is beginning to wake. This has spurred the circle to demand more sacrifices for the blood rites in the Sentinel Woods.

entries.name: Kandar
entries: Kandar is home to the three major clans—{@book Limgri|GrimHollowPG24|8|Clan Limgri}, {@book Mithra|GrimHollowPG24|8|Clan Mithra}, and {@book Morgöng|GrimHollowPG24|8|Clan Morgöng}—who are opposed to Thrull and the Prismatic Circle's theocratic control. The united clans of Kandar keep watch for incursions along the region's disputed plain of tundra spreading down from the Foxwood Range.

In the far east, the converted monastery of Fort Kentigern is now the last shield against encroaching waves of coldfire spreading across the ice bridge from the Volgen peninsula. The coldfire threat is dire, with many seeing it as an omen of doom and perhaps the return of Gormadraug. The Order of Kentigern welcomes all within the fort, making it a haven for reformed criminals and exiles willing to battle the coldfire threat.

Solheim sits on Shadow Storm Gulf and is the only notable settlement of Clan Limgri. It is the smallest of Kandar's three cities and was once the religious center of Valika. It is home to the Sun Temple, the city's most beautiful structure, which was abandoned after the fall of the gods. Some claim the temple's walls reveal secrets when the sun's fleeting summer rays shine across its runic inscriptions. Today, only the city's shipyards grant a modicum of pride to its residents, where an atmosphere of doom pervades even during the longest summer days.

Hrist is the cultural center of Kandar and its largest and most cosmopolitan city, welcoming merchants and diplomats from the southern lands. It is the seat of Clan Mithra, with Queen Andrea Helsing ruling from the Great Hall's Sommer Throne. The city is also home to the Skald Library, whose collections are among the greatest in all of Etharis. It notably holds several pristine copies of the Lord Edda, the codified oral legends of the Valikan clans.

Further south is Summerhelm, Kandar's second-largest city and the seat of Clan Morgöng. It is generally accepted as Kandar's southernmost extent, despite Runeheim being even further south. Summerhelm's construction is reminiscent of the southern kingdoms, particularly the Summer Palace, which is built like a typical Ostoyan castle. Tall shadows from massive outer walls give the city a shadowy atmosphere, which suits the illicit drug trade prospering within.

Skuggiskogr Forest's shadowy pines spread from Summerhelm to Shadow Storm Gulf. Few enter the woods for fear of the fey who are said to hide within. These dark trees press against the Grey Spine Mountains, physically separating Valika from the sunless lands of Ostoya to the south.

entries.name: Volgen
entries: Though the north poses uncountable dangers, many fear the most significant threat is coldfire spreading from the Volgen peninsula. This phenomenon resembles bluish flames that burn on ice, snow, plants, and animals. Anything it meets burns like oil. Water cannot douse it; only the hottest flames can put it out.

Frozen Cinnabar is a recent casualty of coldfire, a once-vibrant fishing community now encased in ice. A rescue mission sent from Fort Kentigern failed to return. Most believe the village is now haunted by ghosts seeking vengeance on the living.

entries.name: Cinderghast
entries: Cinderghast is comprised of several volcanic islands north of Kandar. Legends speak of a time when settlements flourished with fertile soil, rich crops, and a happy people. That was, until Rune of the Seven Heroes trapped the salamander Ixlalu, after which the land erupted to become today's poisoned landscape. It is widely believed that the land is cursed, though a few scholars have braved the journey, discovering vast ruins covered in ash and rock.

entries.name: The Clans
entries: In Valika, six major clans have survived for countless generations, each with a unique culture derived from one of Kentigern's band of heroes. Some cling to the old ways of raiding and thralldom, while others now rely on trade, diplomacy, and espionage to gain what they need.

The clans of {@book Thrull|GrimHollowPG24|8|Thrull}—Clans Rune, Sýr, and Völgr—fervently follow the Prismatic Circle and its Grand Druid Hjadana. The Circle preaches that conflict, raiding, and bloodshed are needed to keep the Great Prismatic Wyrm quiescent. Failing to do so will awaken Gormadraug, heralding the world's end.

The major clans of {@book Kandar|GrimHollowPG24|8|Kandar}—Clans Mithra, Morgöng, and Limgri—do not follow the Prismatic Circle's dictates. Though they still raid, these clans see greater opportunities in trade with the south, which clashes with the teachings of the Prismatic Order's Grand Druid. Raids against Kandar from Thrull have escalated, forcing the eastern clans to create standing armies and erect fortifications to protect against incursion.

entries.title: Valikan Clan Emblems (Row 1, L-R) Clan Sýr—Clan Mithra—Clan Limgri (Row 2, L-R) Clan Völgr—Clan Morgöng—Clan Rune

entries.name: Clan Rune
entries: Magic lies at the heart of Clan Rune. Its settlements are sheltered by spellcraft, and its children are watched carefully for hints of magical talent. The settlement's masters teach anyone demonstrating such ability.

As with all Valikan clans, large numbers of Rune folk become warriors, with some being capable arcanists focusing on defense and battle. For those without arcane skills, wielding runestones provides a satisfactory compromise.

entries.name: Ordun Doransson
entries: The chief of Clan Rune is a powerful mage, claiming the title after his mother's death some fifteen years ago. He is both a formidable rune mage and a potent war chief. He is patient and careful with his actions. Most suspect he has visions of being anointed Keeper of the Three Tribes, but thus far, he has avoided raising the ire of Clan Völgr's leader.

entries.name: Clan Sýr
entries: The most legendary metalworkers of the north hail from Clan Sýr. At ten years of age, clan members are bonded to the Järnförning, a hierarchy of labor, responsibility, and authority that shapes the lives of all clanfolk. The Järnförning is both a guild and a school, set up as twelve tiers of knowledge and skill running from apprentice to clan master.

Most Sýr settlements rise around iron, copper, tin, silver, gold, and rarer adamantine deposits. The clan produces its share of capable warriors. However, the defense of settlements is generally left to bonded thralls and the cunning mechanical traps afforded by the clan's mastery of metalwork.

entries.name: Brokyr Bladesinger
entries: Once a dwarf thrall captured as a child, Brokyr rose through the ranks of Borgund's metalsmiths. She eventually earned the title of sarvif of Clan Sýr thanks to the sublime and deadly beauty of her metalsmithing. She remains dedicated to raising the clan's metalworking skill and has been vocal about reducing the amount of Sýr goods smuggled to southern markets.

entries.name: Clan Völgr
entries: Clan Völgr's hierarchy is established by dominance, with the strongest and hardiest inevitably rising to positions of authority. But strength is reckoned in many ways among the Völgr. Even the most powerful warriors often willingly pay fealty to figures with weaker martial attributes who have proven leadership and tactical acumen.

Chief Nolgr Magnusson, who has ruled the clan for nearly thirty years, stands atop the clan. He is also Keeper of the Three Tribes, a title bestowed by the {@book Prismatic Circle|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Prismatic Circle}, which effectively makes him master of all Thrull-based clans.

entries.name: Nolgr Magnusson
entries: The towering chief of Clan Völgr is nearing fifty, yet appears to retain all the strength and passion of his youth. He has a close relationship with Hjadana, the Grand Druid of the Prismatic Circle. The Circle invested Nolgr as Keeper of the Three Tribes, effectively making him the leader of all of Thrull. Though his loyal warriors continue to sing his praises, a few quietly whisper that their chief does not strike with the same ferocity as he once did.

entries.name: Clan Mithra
entries: Clan Mithra is the most cosmopolitan and inviting to people from beyond Valika's borders. The clan views themselves as a force for order in the surrounding chaos, embodying the legacy of Mithra of the Silver Harp by embracing musical ability and world-spanning knowledge.

entries.name: Queen Andrea Helsing
entries: The clan is governed by their elected leader, Queen Andrea. She has allied with the other Kandar clans to protect against increasing Thrull incursions and is the war chief of the alliance. Preferring diplomacy, Queen Andrea still hopes that there can be a solution to the conflict with their western clans beyond the continued loss of life. Thus far, violence seems to be the only answer their foes will accept.

entries.name: Clan Morgöng
entries: Despite being allied with the more enlightened clans of Kandar, Clan Morgöng still regularly engages in summer raids and the taking of thralls from southern lands, particularly from Ostoyan coastal villages. These thralls often find a better life in Valika than what they faced under the Crimson Court's oppression. As such, many join the clan once their term is complete, making Clan Morgöng even more diverse than Clan Mithra. This diversity also allows the clan to send spies across Etharis, letting them deal in secrets more than any other export.

entries.name: Jarl Birger av Summerhelm
entries: Clan Morgöng lacks anything resembling a centralized government, though Jarl Summerhelm holds a hereditary title as ruler of the clan's capital. He is often absent, more interested in expanding his influence in foreign lands than overseeing the city.

entries.name: Clan Limgri
entries: Clan Limgri is the most nomadic of the six major tribes.

Hospitality is the clan's one governing law. All that they have is shared with those encountered in the wilderness. As a result, the Limgri are frequently the victims of Völgr raids, who take advantage of the clan's generosity.

entries.name: Jarl Angrboða Grata and Taala Brightstar
entries: Outsiders believe Jarl Angrboða and her advisor, Taala Brightstar, speak for the clan. This is mostly true, though the jarl and druid have little impact over how Limgri communities operate; the two are just the most in touch with the clan's elders. In the face of increased Thrull incursions, and unwilling to change their clan's cultural laws, the two petitioned Queen Andrea to extend Mithra outposts further north. In exchange, Clan Limgri agreed to control Cold Iron Keep, which houses the most dangerous, non-Valikan criminals in Kandar.

entries.name: Lesser Clans
entries: Though the six major clans dominate life in Valika, dozens of smaller clans exist as scattered enclaves across the north. These clans range in size and strength from small settlements of less than twenty souls to ones that might soon grow to rival the leading six.
Jarl Summerhelm's away again.
Must be the cold's getting to him.

entries.name: The City of Liesech
entries: Liesech is a perfect port, primarily reached by boat. Bite Bay's open waters and deep-sea ridge allow vessels to anchor closer to the shore. Transiting ships can skirt the coastlines in inclement weather, but ship captains must be vigilant. The turbulent currents of the surrounding waters have been known to throw vessels off course into sea stacks, exciting sea monsters rumored to dwell in the nearby area called the "Eye of the Sea."

Liesech's many channels and ditches keep tide swells at bay. Landforms corral coastal winds, keeping temperatures mild and warm. Waning sea storms in warmer periods deliver light rains that barely supports agriculture. Outsiders opine the sparse rainfall is due to the haze hanging over the city. Locals simply shrug their shoulders when asked.

entries.name: History
entries: Liesecher trace their origins to Otto Bouwengracht, whose family was influential when it was just a large fishing village. Ambitious and daring, Bouwengracht fished beyond safe waters, where he hoped to net larger catches. Before long, he became a wealthy merchant, with his family and those who trusted him rising to prominence. Liesech prospered under his leadership. He constructed a remarkable fleet of sailing ships. He sent his fleet to establish trading routes across the Emerald Sea and further. Liesech continued to grow, and with the revered Bouwengracht family as its voice, Liesech pronounced itself a free city.

entries.name: The Death of the Bouwengrachts and Rise of the Countess
entries: One day, the Bouwengracht family, along with other noble families, were found brutally killed—the blood entirely drained from their bodies. Almost immediately, the threats from Bite Bay and the Eye of the Sea returned. Terrible tides brought forth monsters that overran the docks. Destructive swells of flotsam and waste rolled into Liesech's shores—toppling buildings, drowning many, and delivering undulating, many-mouthed and tentacled monsters who consumed any in their path.

A savior stepped forward in the form of a recently arrived noble: Analita von Raiza marshalled troops and used magic to fight the incursion. On several occasions, the woman sailed out toward the Eyes of the Sea to find the source of the trouble.
After many days of turmoil and death, the seas calmed. The newly crowned Countess von Raiza returned and established her home at the {@book High Belfry|GrimHollowPG24|8|Notable Locations}.

entries.name: The Weeping Pox
entries: In the days following the conflict at the Eye of the Sea, Liesechers thought the worst was over. They were wrong. Disease swept through Liesech in the form of the Weeping Pox. The symptoms and consequences of the disease were like Etharis had never seen.

Days following exposure, the victim expresses hyper-vigilance and excitability, followed by bouts of delirium and fever. In its final stages, flesh takes on a bone-white pallor, and two bright red pustules appear on the neck. Right before death, melancholia sweeps over the victim as it weeps all the blood from its body and looses a final, powerful, painful wail.
The Weeping Pox claimed thousands.

In time, allied aid arrived in the form of the {@book Morbus Doctore|GrimHollowPG24|10|Morbus Doctore}. Their arrival sparked hope, but they could not fully stem the tide of death—only offer some relief to the pain. The masked countenances of the medically trained doctors remain a constant reminder of the death and danger that strike down citizens of Liesech every day.

entries.name: The Liesecher Way
entries: Most Liesechers make their way from their homes to their fishing boats and businesses after five morning bell tolls from the High Belfry. After six evening bell tolls, business dealings move from district streets to inside buildings. Those folks who are out beyond six bells are believed to be employed by the {@book Golden Barge Company|GrimHollowPG24|8|Important Factions}—or one of the foolish that risk becoming an addition to the accepted nightly disappearances.

The Weeping Pox is a part of everyday life in Liesech, and the populace does whatever is necessary to keep up their spirits in the midst of death and misery. Locals call the Pox a necessity. They insist it prevents overpopulation and keeps visitors fearful from staying too long. They note with feigned relief how the disease spares children—although the children still bear its disfiguring marks and spread the disease like anyone else.

Despite many losses, it has not claimed the city's revered Countess von Raiza, who the locals say miraculously managed trade to break the Bürach Empire's oppressive blockade. Citizens point to the arrival of the Morbus Doctore, who aid the sick and troubled.

entries.name: Countess Analita Von Raiza
entries: Liesech's interim leader has become an enigmatic figure since rescuing Liesech from the sea-spawned threats after the death of the city's ruling family. When she first arrived in the city, she was constantly seen in public, attending events frequently. Her beauty and charm were renowned, and her popularity in high society rivaled even the Bouwengrachts.

In the wake of the terrible attacks, she has taken on the mantle of leadership but has not appeared in public. In the face of the Weeping Pox, the citizenry appreciates what she's done and how she's been able to keep order, but they wonder about her condition.

entries.name: Cult of the Black Lady
entries: Countess von Raiza has not been seen in public since her battle with the mysterious power that lives in the Eye of the Sea. Her orders are carried out by a group that claims to work on her behalf: the Cult of the Black Lady.

They move about the city quickly and with purpose. Few are brave enough to interact with the members of this group unless there is a compelling reason. Their reputation for efficiency and power leads many to believe that little good can come from such interactions.

entries.name: Golden Barge Company (G.B.C.)
entries: The members of the Golden Barge Company are committed to the economic prosperity of Liesech. They have come to understand the importance of commerce, and the power they wield serves that commerce. They are not afraid to remove obstacles to free trade through means that might cause others to blanch. They employ thieves and assassins when necessary, chalking it up to the greater good.

They know the city streets, sewers, and waterways well and use the most expedient routes to complete their tasks. Few among their leaders suspect that the Red Mourning and the Weeping Pox events are related, and trust that Aphaleon reveals the truth about these tragedies in time.

entries.name: Morbus Doctore
entries: The Morbus Doctore formed shortly after the Weeping Pox claimed its first victims. Healers and apothecaries from the city were at a loss, and they reached out to colleagues and contacts from across Etharis. Slowly, help arrived.

Before long, the presence of these healers was felt. Wearing dark long coats, hats, and bird-like masks, they solemnly set to task. A city area was cleared, and high iron walls erected around it. This quarantined area is called Zielenhaus.

Behind Zielenhaus walls, the Morbus Doctore bring the Weeping Pox afflicted. Painful examinations and extractions take place, often with the same result: death. Though few are cured of the disease, suffering is lessened. They contain the suffering behind their walls. They treat the sick with reverent respect. They play with the children. They offer hope for a cure of the Weeping Pox.

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: Eye of the Sea. At the center of Bright Bay are dark, turbulent waters where, historically, ship crews that failed to heed warnings were lost and never seen again. Yet the area's mystery continues to be the stuff of minstrel musing and tavern tales. Within its waters are believed to be anything from utopian settlements of enchanting sea creatures, to gilded schools of fish that can feed families for a lifetime, to great treasures left behind by the death of the old gods.

entries.name: The High Belfry
entries: This multi-story manse housed the entire Bouwengracht clan, with a council hall and a chapel that also served as the city's bell tower. The manse was renovated to accommodate Countess Analita von Raiza and her servitors. The High Belfry has visitors on special occasions, particularly during the Week of the Grand Citywide Market, but those visits are kept to public areas of the mansion, council hall, and church.

entries.name: Zielenhaus
entries: The Morbus Doctore's iron-walled compound encompasses a four-block region. Within its many dark-stoned structures are a labyrinth of cells, examination rooms, laboratories, libraries, and vaults filled with instruments the Morbus Doctore need to carry out their efforts against the Weeping Pox.

entries.name: The City of Morencia
entries: The city-state of Morencia is located on small islands in the Gulf of Lions, west of the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces} and east of the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Charneault Kingdom}. Morencia has maintained its independence since its founding, resisting the might of the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire} and, more recently, the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces}.

The most important topographical feature that enabled this defense is the {@book Misty Reefs|GrimHollowPG24|8|Notable Locations}. A supernatural phenomenon that puts all vessels at risk, this barrier has prevented nearly every naval attack on Morencia.

Morencia's protective features make it a nautical, and therefore an economic, powerhouse. Few merchants can claim to be an international player without having their goods interact in some way with Morencia.

entries.name: History
entries: The unnatural fog wall that rolls across the seascape can only be navigated by experienced captains with access to magical powers. Alternatively, captains supplied with a magical {@i Nautantlicus}, obtained from Morencia's Navigator's Guild, can do the same.

Morencia's original settlers—whose culture, beliefs, magical prowess, and ways of life were challenged by the strictures of the Bürachers—fled that land in search of safe haven from subjugation. To fully escape, they had to traverse those mists. They did so only after losing countless friends and family, but their legacy remains alive.
The original settlers found dark, swampy islands. Once ashore, they built homes, bridges, and industry that has grown to its current state.

entries.name: The Battle of the Castinellan Armada
entries: Throughout its history, Castinella attempted to conquer or destroy Morencia's people, militarily and politically. Morencian citizens hear stories of the cruelty and depravities of the {@book Arcanist Inquisition|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Arcanist Inquisition}, and they vow to never let its influence reach their shores. In return, the Inquisitors view Morencia as not just a cauldron of arcane power waiting to explode, but also as a cesspool of excess and frivolity.

Recently, the Castinellan armada captured a Morencian ship captain. They attempted to intimidate the captain into safely guiding the armada through the Misty Reefs. If not for the vigilant fisherfolk of >Charneault who saw the potential invasion and got word to Morencia's authorities, Morencia might not have been able to foil the plot.

entries.name: The Morencian Way
entries: The people attempt to embody the virtues of their ancestors: determined, resilient, hard-working, and family-oriented. They understand that sacrifice for the community is honorable and that fair trade is essential. Morencians are naturally cautious. They don't need to be reminded of their ancestors' hardships.

Some hardships are harder to forget. It is believed that ancestral spirits visit relatives in their dreams, never allowing them to forget their suffrage. Those who endure these unconscious reminders are pitied by others and discreetly consoled with a hand on the shoulder and a phrase, "Thank you for suffering."

entries.name: Districts
entries: Morencia is organized into five main districts, with four surrounding the Government District, each reachable by bridge or canal. Arsenal inspectors at each checkpoint scrutinize everyone passing between districts.

entries.name: Arbrumel (Harbor District)
entries: Docks of varying size accommodate arriving ships. Local vessels with proper authorizations are given cursory inspections, and Morencian cutters quickly guide foreign ships to the quarantine bay waters. Boat and dockside vendors welcome arrivals with foodstuffs and ominous warnings of recent events. Flotsam scourers use skiffs to scavenge debris from vessels sunk by the Misty Reefs for anything of value. Dark smoke plumes rise from the Arsenal of Morencia's shipyards. Murders of crows roost atop dark steel walls that surround the area.

entries.name: Vueami (Guest District)
entries: The taverns, inns, and fest halls here offer a range of comforts for Morencia's visitors. City representatives live in satellite house-offices, where they meet business contacts or review trade offers. Business owners learn what they can about guests to help the city filter good business dealings from bad. Identified fraudsters are taken to Arsenal jails and never heard from again. Morencian greeters meet other visitors, offering guided tours, goods both legal and illegal, and recommendations for any other service one might imagine.

entries.name: Zitoean (Citizen District)
entries: The majority of Morencian citizens live here. Numbers and statues carved into building foundations identify residential blocks. Stores and small vendor tents line most walkways and canals. Businesses operating in Ke 'Centre have shops here: citizens need not go far to resupply, and at lower prices than elsewhere.

entries.name: Ke 'Centre (Business District)
entries: The prominent {@book Augustine Trading Company|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Augustine Trading Company}, the Banker's Guild, and the Navigator's Guild are headquartered here. Travel is slow to impossible—taking hours to get from place to place. Citizens know when company or guildmasters travel: their dark wooden skiffs dart through canals. Foreigners, ambassadors, and businesspeople mingle here. Visitors are watched carefully but are free to travel as long as they can offer a good reason when stopped and questioned by Arsenal sentries.

entries.name: Enducour (Government District)
entries: From the bridge checkpoints of the Water Strider Bridge, the Supreme Council's Hall are clearly visible, as are the spires of the Maegilate, the city's arcanum center. This is Morencia's smallest district, where only matters of governance and academia occur. Councilmembers, professors, students, and guests arrive from other districts to conduct their daily business here.

entries.name: Doge Lorenzo Flabenici
entries: Only three people have served as the city's official representative. The Doge serves a lifetime term. Traditionally, the election process is several weeks long. Flabenici's election occurred in mere days, so quickly that some Morencians saw this as a sign of hope. Others proclaimed this an omen of darker days to come.

{@b Kohlys Papinian, "The Blazing Brain."} Most Morencians readily admit that the personality dubbed "The Blazing Brain" is the real power in Morencia. As the Banker's Guild leader, she allowed the Augustine Trading Company to acquire a majority stake in the guild, while simultaneously acquiring a majority stake in the Augustine Trading Company through proxy. This clever ploy gave Kohlys the insight and influence to write loans and secure trade deals.

entries.name: The Supreme Council
entries: Morencia's main governing body is the city-state's highest legal and political authority. It consisted of ten seats, nine of which are occupied by Morencia's guildmasters, the Maegilate Headmaster, and other business and military leaders. Recently opened citizen representative seats have expanded the council's number to twenty.

The council is always led by Morencia's current Doge. This structure allows for the consideration and influence from each corner of Morencian society. While intrigue and self-interest roil through the council, some leaders have the best interests of Morencia's citizens at heart.

entries.name: The Navigator's Guild
entries: Deeply rooted in this guild's history is the mystery of the Misty Reefs and the secrets of Morencian magic. The fusion of the two is the key to navigating the mist and its dangers. This knowledge is deeply guarded, and costly to maintain. Morencian allies with this knowledge carry an enchanted Nautantlicus, a sextant magically imbued with a route through the mists. This enchantment is temporary and monitored by the Maegilate.

entries.name: The Banker's Guild
entries: The guild's influence is apparent. It is the most prominent money-lending entity in Morencia. As such, it provides loans to citizens, guilds, and government, with privately negotiated terms. Guild vaults offer asset protection bolstered by the Misty Reefs and magically warded by the Maegilate. The merger with the Augustine Trading Company expands their reach throughout Etharis and allows for investment beyond the mists.

entries.name: The Maegilate
entries: The first gatherings of the city's magically talented settlers evolved into this teaching body. Selected citizens master their ancestors' magic. Arcane academia also includes city service: guild consulting, Supreme Council escorts, Arsenal support, and duties within the Maegilate itself. The Magi Profesorie oversees the city's arcane interests and is the institute's voice on the Supreme Council.

entries.name: The Misty Reefs
entries: Morencia's ghostly mist wall is spotted miles out by vessel lookouts. This is more than enough time to avoid it, or if entering, using the Nautantlicus given to the vessel by Morencia's Navigator's Guild. If the magical sextant isn't closely heeded, vessels know quickly enough. They can become lost in the misty labyrinth, wreck upon jagged reefs and sandbars, or be attacked by spectral spirits of sunken ships.

entries.name: The Arsenal of Morencia
entries: All Morencians proudly boast that their navy is the most formidable in all Etharis. Few contest this. The mighty galleons, sleek cutters, and skulker skiffs are made with the finest craftsmanship. The fleet is spread along Morencia's shorelines. Its largest presence floats in the waters just outside the Arsenal shipyard walls.

name: Chapter 9: Magic in Etharis
entries: In Etharis, "magic" is a catch-all term for a power outside of mortal ability, a means of altering reality altogether. In truth, there are three separate forms of magic, each with their own mystical sources: arcane, divine, and primal.

Magic in the Grim Hollow setting is relatively safe for those who have the proper training and good sense to use it wisely. However, even the most careful practitioner of magic risks falling victim to its corrupting and unpredictable powers. Casting spells, being the target of benevolent magic, or using magic-infused items might be safe for decades, until one small miscalculation or unfortunate coincidence unleashes terrible consequences.

Magical transformations, vile curses, or supernatural attention are within the realm of possible outcomes from prolonged magic use. Then there's the superstitious folks and devoted inquisitors—for whom the very wielding is at best a reason to be wary, and at worst an unforgivable sin.
The power of magic, however, is an irresistible temptation. When one has little to lose, great risks become the only viable option. And that's when the unthinkable might happen.
Ulmyr's Gate, the Gods, there are so many ways it can go wrong—and that many more that it can go very right.

entries.name: Arcane Magic
entries: The archmage pours over a tome in his school. The itinerate wizard travels the world on a quest for ancient knowledge. The resolute sorcerer, haunted with power, turns the fire that burns inside them upon a snarling monster. Each of these rely on a latent force that exists throughout Etharis. Commoners call it magic. Those who can wield it call it the arcane. Despite the innumerable books written on the subject, the source of this power is not well understood.

The greatest authority in Etharis on arcane magic was a man named Raakan el-Adakar. An archmage who lived during the early Age of Expansion, Raakan built an impressive library on the nature and source of magic. Much of the modern literature concerning the arcane is rooted in Raakan's research.

He determined that arcane magic emanates from a dimension parallel to the Material Plane, a place suffused with energy that can alter reality. He named this plane "Numena" and the energy itself "numen." Magic, according to Raakan, involves using numen to give physical shape and form to the magician's will.

Commoners with the right gifts or knowledge can touch, or according to Raakan, "speak to" the realm Numena to elicit a response. The language used to communicate might require a spoken phrase, a complex gesture, or a particular object. These combinations of verbal, somatic, and material components are called "spells." When mages cast spells, they weaken the border separating Numena from the Material Plane in order to draw and shape numen into a magical effect as woven by the spell.

Wizards learn the language of spells through study and years of careful practice. Learning to speak to the arcane is immensely difficult, so that even the most powerful wizards must utilize a spellcasting focus and a spellbook. Spells more complicated than cantrips require persistent study from the pages of a spellbook to ensure the components of the spell are performed accurately each day.

Those capable of casting spells without study or memorization are called sorcerers. For these casters, touching Numena is like flexing a muscle. For others, called warlocks, it's as if the knowledge or arcane spells is whispered into their minds from an unknown entity beyond the Material Plane.

Several theories have been put forth on what Numena actually is. One states that Numena is a word encompassing any realm other than the Material Plane. This hypothesis attempts to reconcile perceived differences between arcane, divine, and primal magic, arguing the process of casting a spell is the same for each, except for where the numen originates.

Other scholars believe Numena isn't a realm at all. Some suggest it's an invisible force not dissimilar to gravity, but which is imperceptible to all besides mages. Another theory infers that Numena is itself a living entity attempting to shape our dimension to match its own. This premise, though terrifying, explains why spells are so difficult to keep in mind: knowledge of their components slips from the mind like oily aberrations.

entries.name: Societal Mistrust
entries: Sadly, Raakan el-Adakar did not live to complete his research. His own thesis was that Numena was a traversable realm, and for his final magical experiment he opened a portal to some magical plane. When Raakan returned some years later from whichever realm he'd visited, be it Numena or some other distant reality, he was unintelligible. He spent his final days confined in Cold Iron Keep under the watchful eye of guards, who claimed to suffer bizarre visions and disturbing ailments until the day Raakan perished.

Society has always had a divided and suspicious view of arcane magic. Though its power is unrivalled, its mystery, unpredictability, and undeniable dangers make it feared and hated by those who cannot wield it. To rulers, magic represents a threat to their authority. To commoners, mages are a mutual enemy who can be blamed for all the world's unexplained troubles.

There is certainly merit in fearing the arcane. Raakan el-Adakar's fate is one among countless cautionary tales. Casting spells is fundamentally the practice of trying to speak a language nobody truly understands, and the unintended results of experimentation have been devastating, such as when countless souls were lost in the instant the mages of Ulmyr's Gate upended their city into the Ethereal Plane.

Even among arcane scholars there are fears that magic permanently thins the veil between the Material Plane and Numena. Each spell cast is like picking at the threads of a tapestry until holes begin to form. If true, what horrors could have wriggled through? This anxiety is the basis of a theory that spellcasters are to blame for the advent of the Great Beast. Though entirely unproven, such a rumor has made those who wield magic even further detested by society.

Characters capable of wielding magic should be wary of when and where they choose to demonstrate their talents. Even friendly acquaintances may turn fearful or even hostile when confronted with the arcane. To a mage, the misunderstandings of the uneducated can be as dangerous as any magic.

A mortician willing to help a murder investigation might flee in terror upon witnessing a wizard speak with the dead. A bard's audience could quickly turn into an angry mob if they believe they've been enchanted by a bardic spell.

A wise mage's greatest asset is caution. Rumors of their magic could easily reach the ears of a truly dangerous foe, such as an agent of the {@book Arcanist Inquisition|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Arcanist Inquisition}. However, cultures across Etharis do treat their spellcasters with differing attitudes. In some corners of the continent a mage may yet find sanctuary.

entries.name: Regional Attitudes
entries: In the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire}, magic is considered a means to project power, instill obedience, gather information, and maintain order. For these reasons, magic is highly valued, yet mages themselves are heavily regulated and their freedom restricted.

Those who cannot cast magic fear it. Therefore, strict laws govern the lives of mages across the empire. Licenses are given to mages who graduate their college, which stipulates not only which spells they're permitted to cast but also under what circumstances. Sorcerers and warlocks who operate outside official sanctions are outlaws and may become hunted if they're feared to be dangerous.

With the coming of the Great Beast, magic is now treated with even greater suspicion, as some have come to suspect that the entity was the result of an arcane experiment gone horribly wrong. The Hearthkeepers have not denied this notion, as it deflects their own culpability on the matter.

The {@book Ostoyan|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Ostoyan Empire} view of magic is more varied. In the provinces still under the sway of the {@book Crimson Court|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Crimson Court}, the lower classes, still incapable of understanding the arcane, fear magic. They stay clear of magic and its practitioners to avoid inciting the jealousy and ire of the nobility.

The Crimson Court makes it clear to all its subjects that the use of magic is theirs by right; anyone who uses magic must either become a servant of the Crimson Court or be labeled as a threat and destroyed. Thus, one most often finds Ostoyan mages in the Crimson Court's employ taking part in wars against the rebellious province of Raevo and the Bürach Empire.

In the province of Raevo, magic is by and large an accepted part of daily life. The workshops and markets of Castalore host wizards leading around magical constructs or trading occult objects among themselves. All this is done under the watchful eye of the {@book Ravencourt Sanctuary|GrimHollowPG24|9|Ravencourt Sanctuary}. Magic is valued to maintain sovereignty against the power of the Crimson Court and the forces of the Bürach Empire, as well as to repel raids from the marauding clans of Valika.

In the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Charneault Kingdom}, the elves treat magic as an integral part of their lives. They hold skilled mages in high esteem, even as they tread cautiously around the cost of seeking greater magical power. While elven magecraft has achieved great magical benefits for its people, the price for missteps is often paid by the entirety of the kingdom, as is the case with the dark mist.

In the human-controlled lands of Charneault, attitudes vary greatly. Mages are treated with respect in more learned circles, but among Knight Chapters they are met with suspicion, and in the case of the Severe Templars, outright hostility. These human-centric forces hold elven magic accountable for the suffering endured by the populace, leading them to forego most arcane abilities in favor of strength in arms and divine fervor.

It's worth noting the {@book Castinellan Provinces'|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces} stance toward magic: deplorably, the theocracy is hostile to all forms of arcane or primal magic. It is the will of {@book Empyreus|GrimHollowPG24|9|Empyreus}, they claim, that their people wage a righteous war against all magic users and those who harbor them.

To undertake this task, the {@book Arcanist Inquisition|GrimHollowPG24|10|The Arcanist Inquisition} has assembled a host of knights and common soldiers under the command of clerics and paladins. Backed by the power of belief in the inherent corruptive nature of arcane magic, they call on divine magic to locate mages and neutralize them.

As such, magic-using arcanists must be careful when entering and traveling with the Castinellan Provinces, lest they be arrested, imprisoned, and very likely put to death by the Arcanist Inquisition. Mages cannot expect sympathy from the common folk, as most peasants hold all magic as witchcraft and devilry, the power of demons brought to Etharis.

The Castinellans have yet to move against nations that employ magic, such as the Bürach Empire and the Charneault Kingdom. But should the theocracy grow more powerful, a "righteous war" would certainly be in the cards. It would only take one international incident of arcane treachery—real, imagined, or staged—to incite a holy war between Castinella and a neighboring nation.

entries.name: Colleges of Magic
entries: A force as potentially dangerous as magic has the ability to kill the powerful, alter the destiny of nations, and even destroy worlds. With that ever-present danger in mind, authorities across the nations of Etharis attempt to keep magic regulated to some degree.

One method of regulation is to control the teaching of magic via colleges, schools, universities, or other institutions of learning. In addition to memorizing spells and arcane theory, pupils are taught to respect the social hierarchy their nation places a mage within.
The most prevalent and relevant of the arcane colleges are listed here.

entries.name: Erlefurt Colleges
entries: Western Nordenland is the center for arcane learning in the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire}. The leaders of the nation wish to make their capital city, Erlefurt, the preeminent locale for magic users and other arcanists in Etharis. They have a saying: "Keep your friends close, your enemies closer, and the wizards safely under your thumb."

The capital city of Erlefurt has the highest number of magical colleges anywhere in the continent, with the Magister College being the oldest and most venerable of all. There is a healthy, and sometimes unhealthy, competition between the various colleges to recruit and retain the most promising students and the most powerful and revered instructors.

It's difficult for an aspiring mage to enter any of the Erlefurt Colleges, but the Magister College proves itself to be exceptionally stringent. The Magister chooses only the best among its applicants—not merely those with high magical aptitude, but also those most likely to bring greater honor to the school.

The Erlefurt Colleges see magic as a force that must be harnessed with wisdom, the kind that only those trained in the way of the Erlefurt Mages possess. As such, all students must swear to follow a set of rigid laws, listed in a tome called the {@i Magician's Codex}.
The primary laws in the Codex include the following:

items: Only mages who pass their final tests of the Erlefurt Colleges can carry the title of Erlefurt Mage.
An Erlefurt Mage shall defend their College against all who would despoil them or steal their secrets.
Only the Erlefurt Colleges may train a student. All students must be registered and approved by the Colleges.
All magical experiments must be registered and approved by the Colleges.

entries: Any Erlefurt wizard who breaks these codes is hunted down by the Enforcers, a group of elite abjurers highly adept at subduing one of their own. Once captured, offenders are taken to the Magician's Court and judged by the Grand Mage. If found guilty, transgressors may be magically imprisoned or even executed.

As for other arcane institutions who don't subscribe to these tenets, they are treated with contempt and even outright hostility. On the rare occasion that a mage trained at another college is deemed worthy to grace the halls of an Erlefurt college, they are watched carefully and forced to swear to the{@i Magician's Codex}.

As there are no formal magical colleges in the Grarjord or other parts of Valika, members of the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans} send promising students to Erlefurt for tutelage. Valikan students are considered oddities by their Bürach peers, even more so than students hailing from other nations of Etharis.

entries.name: Ravencourt Sanctuary
entries: East of Raevo's shores, hidden among the mists of its enchanted isle, the Ravencourt Sanctuary trains its mages in its own academy. Unlike its rival the Magister College, the Sanctuary believes that magic may only grow through freedom. Thus, the Ravencourt Sanctuary has few laws to guide the actions of its mages. Their primary rule is that the mages must keep all the secrets of the Sanctuary safe. Apart from that, a mage who successfully graduates from the Sanctuary is free to do what they will and teach whomever they want.

Despite their seeming lack of laws, the punishment for breaking the Sanctuary's primary rule is both harsh and unequivocal. Before they leave the Sanctuary, a Ravencourt mage swears a vow to protect their academy's secrets. They write their name on a page of an enchanted book, finishing their signature with a drop of their own blood. This creates a highly potent curse: if the graduate should break the vow, the page tears itself from the book and burns. The mage consequently dies an unspeakable death.

Mages from {@book Erlefurt|GrimHollowPG24|9|Erlefurt Colleges} view those trained at the Ravencourt Sanctuary as dangerous occultists. Though their talents are undeniable, the apparent freedom with which Ravencourt magicians wield their magic marks them as a threat to the cautious spellcasters of Erlefurt. The secrecy of the Sanctuary also incites rumors of daemonology and pact-making within its walls.

The two colleges are technically at war. Mages from either side have standing orders to annihilate each other on sight, though this is rarely practiced. If the current stalemate between Bürach and Ostoya were nudged once more into war, perhaps members of the two mage schools could be propelled into open conflict.

entries.name: Sarmar Academy
entries: Having lived for thousands of years as a people, the elves are no beginners when it comes to magic. In their capital of Tol Leyemil, they have established their own college for wizards: Sarmar Academy, famous for its bards, mystics, and druids.

The Sarmar mages teach that magic is learned and controlled through careful observation. By watching and listening to the natural world, the elves believe the Spirits of Nature whisper the language of spells to those with the aptitude to listen. The school teaches their students to pay close attention to detail and focus on what they can sense. Before a single cantrip is cast, pupils spend months learning meditation, observation, and introspection. This emphasis on patience makes teaching slow. Magical education at Sarmar can last decades. For elves, of course, that's the blink of an eye—not so for the shorter-lived species.

Being an entirely elven city that forbids non-elves from entering, only elves may enroll in Sarmar Academy. The exception is Aemanyir House, a branch of the Academy located outside the city limits. This program was launched by an elven mage named Alren Corynthios as an experiment in teaching the elven way of magic to non-elves. Most of the Sarmar academics consider this a waste of time, but Alren's persistence and connections with the elven nobility have kept the program going for many years, if just barely.

One area of contention within the Sarmar Academy is its loyalties. The tension between the human rulers of {@book Charneault|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Charneault Kingdom} and the elven leaders of Tol Leyemil makes humans wary of how Sarmar's mages would be used if the elves ever decided to push the question of independence. The presence of Aemanyir House is a slight reassurance to the humans that not all of Sarmar's mages would join a secession movement.

entries.name: Renegade Colleges
entries: There are several unsanctioned or unrecognized colleges in Etharis that claim to teach magic. As they tend to produce subpar mages (according to sanctioned magical schools), they're looked upon with scorn. They're commonly viewed as potential sources of magical mishaps, as without proper guidance, these mages may cause irreparable harm to the reputation of all mages—or worse, bring about a magical catastrophe.

The {@book Erlefurt|GrimHollowPG24|9|Erlefurt Colleges} Mages have made it their protocol to capture any so-called renegade wizards and bring them to Erlefurt for processing and possible reeducation. Those who refuse this polite entreaty are viewed as enemy combatants and dealt with harshly.

Castinella, with its omnipresent {@book Arcanist Inquisition|GrimHollowPG24|9|The Arcanist Inquisition}, has no official arcane college. Underground education centers in the land secretly provide instruction to arcane-sensitive individuals, hoping to help them control and harness their powers before they are noticed by the Inquisitors. While this form of magical education would be considered basic and remedial by the standards of formal colleges, it is no less important to those trying to control their magical abilities within a hostile nation.

Magical training in Valika is handled in a much different manner. Clan Rune treats arcane magic much like any other skill: masters take on apprentices who learn the basics of the craft as well as the moral and social imperatives of using numen. After completing their apprenticeship, young Valikan mages are free to travel to other settlements in Valika to practice. Once they have gained the full experience of a life practicing magic, they might become a master and take on apprentices of their own.

Bardic training in Valika teaches practitioners a different type of arcane magic, with additional instruction in performance, history, mythology, poetics, and more. This training ranges from individual instruction to small schools with a master skald teaching students the skills necessary to act as a bard or skald.
I've heard tell of a priest in Castinella who trained with us long ago. He spends his days teaching gifted youths to hide their talents—a comforting thought if ever there was one.
entries.by: High Speaker of the Ravencourt Sanctuary

entries.name: Divine Magic
entries: Divine magic may seem contingent on the presence of gods, deities, Arch Seraphs, and Arch Daemons, but it is much more nuanced and complex. Divine spellcasters believe their deity sanctions them to draw from the power of the heavens—gifted magic in answer to prayers. Others believe a deity is not necessary to perform miracles, that faith is not so transactional, and that divinity itself chooses the virtuous in times when divine magic is in great need.

Less theological literature suggests a darker truth: a cleric's prayers are no different from a wizard's incantations. Divine spells commune with otherworldly energy and shape it to the caster's will, exactly as arcane magic weaves numen—perhaps the two sources of power are one and the same. Religious orders like the Hearthkeepers decry such theory as blasphemy. Of course, if it were true, by what right would the church limit the magic of others whilst celebrating their own spellcasters as prophets?

Yet as obscure as theology can be, evidence of the divine in Etharis exists. The downcast still remember the heavens as a glorious dream. The Gods' End itself undeniably diminished the light of divine magic across the continent, eradicating the population of divine mages until only a scattered few remained.

Of course, no one who records history witnessed the gods rise or fall—if there were ever gods at all. The cosmology and mythology presented here is just one that can be heard preached in the chapels, churches, and holy places of Etharis. What is seen as gospel in one area could be heretical if preached elsewhere.
But while mortal minds can accept their own lives are fleeting and small, the need to believe in a greater story that gives purpose to existence fills the gaps in knowledge with faith.

entries.name: The Aetheric War and Gods' End
entries: The gods came into being at the start of the Era of Antiquity. They opened their powerful eyes to behold a universe of wonder and chaos, and they were enamored. They wanted to shape it into something that resembled them. But to do that, they needed to impose order.

The gods banded together to form a pantheon. They shaped the firmament and the seas, then the land and all the creatures that followed. They named the land Etharis and created the people to populate it and beasts to roam it.

As these newborn mortals turned their minds to their creators, the gods found an endless font of believers. Their worship lifted the gods to greater heights, securing their ability to turn the wheel of the universe.

entries.name: The Aetheric War
entries: The gods were not alone. At the fringes of their ordered realm lurked the Aether Kindred—incomprehensible beings of pure chaos who predated the gods themselves. The Kindred gnawed at the edges of reality. Occasionally the gods would find a piece of creation in disarray—a star gone missing, an ocean dried up, an entire city disappearing into a void of nothingness. Even the gods, in all their power and insight, could not fathom these discrepancies.

That's because the Aether Kindred had no intent. No desires. They were an active threat to the gods' power by their mere presence, and the gods feared that one day all they had built would be stripped away. They had to strike now, while they still had the faith of their followers and stood at the height of their power. Thus, the gods banded together as a host and attacked the Kindred. This became known as the Aetheric War.

Scriptures differ on what happened to the gods who participated in the war. Some theologians say it was a slaughter. Others believe the gods were consumed by the eldritch horrors they tried to defeat. Others state that some gods retreated from this plane to someplace the Kindred could not reach them. While nothing is certain about what happened to the gods thereafter, it is clear they were utterly defeated.

Of the great pantheon, only the four youngest survived: Galt the Builder, Maligant Warclad, Ulmyr the Wise, and Aurelia of the Hearth. Perhaps out of caution or fear, they held back from joining the other gods in the Aetheric War. Thus, they were the only ones left to pick up the pieces, becoming responsible for the fostering of Etharis' future.

entries.name: The Gods' End
entries: As the surviving gods left to guide the mortals of Etharis, the Four Divines decided to band together. Their idea was to create an ideal empire, the "one above all" to act as a shining beacon for all mortals to emulate. This idea became the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|9|The Bürach Empire}.

Its first emperor, Indorius I, would be graced with the power to become a guiding light for all the nations of Etharis. To this end, the Four Divines gifted him with four holy artifacts that would enable him to rule wisely and protect his empire.

For a time, all was good. Unfortunately, this method of salvation carried the seeds of its own destruction. The Bürach Empire so loved their emperor that they decided to keep his bloodline eternally pure. The Church of the Hearthkeepers imposed the Edict of Eternal Blood, mandating that only those of the line of Indorius I may ascend to the throne. With that, they encouraged his descendants to intermarry, weakening their blood.

The wretched outcome of this practice finally manifested in the Mad Emperor, Leopold I. When he ascended to his legacy and touched the divine artifacts, his mind and soul were instantly connected with the Four Divines—and it drove them all to madness. Galt, Maligant, Ulmyr, and Aurelia warred among themselves, each vying to reign as the One True God of the universe. This culminated in the Gods' End, when these last remaining deities destroyed one another.
Etharis carries on as a godless world. While divine magic remains, it is but a shadow of its former glory.

entries.name: Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons
entries: In the absence of the gods, Arch Seraphs and Arch Daemons took up their mantle. While these beings are lesser divinities, they inherited part of their patron deity's power, making them the perceived source of divine magic in Etharis.

The Arch Seraphs are worshipped for the virtues they embody. Arch Daemons personify apparent vices or evil tendencies. In truth, these celestial beings view themselves as unbiased aspects of the human spirit.

entries.name: Empyreus
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Valor}

The Arch Seraph of Valor is the former servant of the war god Maligant. A favorite of holy warriors, he is the patron of courage and strength, as well as all who seek to wage a righteous war. Due to his uncompromising nature, Empyreus's followers tend to become zealots in the pursuit of righteousness. Empyreus is traditionally opposed by the Lord of Fear, Beleth. Of late, however, Beleth seems to have gotten the better of him, as some of Empyreus's followers have begun using terror to enforce their beliefs.

Crusaders, war clerics, and inquisitors are among those who wear Empyreus's sigils on their armor and shields. Tacticians and champions outside the clergy may also look to Empyreus's example for strength and courage.

entries.name: Zabriel
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Truth}

The Arch Seraph of Truth was once the librarian of the goddess Typharia. Zabriel abandoned her mistress's neutral stance and instead sought to bring an end to the lies that support tyrants and oppressors. She is loved by scholars and truth seekers. She is opposed directly by {@book Venin|GrimHollowPG24|9|Venin}, the Great Deceiver.

Clerics who spread light and knowledge may choose Zabriel as their patron. Her worshippers' commitment to uncompromising truth makes them excellent inquisitors, and natural enemies of spies and illusionists.
Woe then that one war begets another. A clash of wings, leathery and feathered,
over virtues wholly our own.
entries.by: The Seraphs' War: a History of Etharis

entries.name: Aphaeleon
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Temperance}

Aphaeleon holds sway over the domains of peace and spirituality. Once a servant of Myria, Goddess of Joy, he has since decided there is little joy to be found in Etharis, and has taken to becoming the patron of those who seek spiritual balance. Those who follow Aphaeleon's ways find freedom from addictions and worldly desires. He is opposed by the Arch Daemon of Hedonism, {@book Sitri|GrimHollowPG24|9|Sitri}.
A patron of monks, peacemakers, and spiritual healers, Aphaeleon is honored by those who seek conciliation and enlightenment.

entries.name: Solyma
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Justice}

A truly ancient Arch Seraph, Solyma has remained faithful to the ways of her former master and seeks to destroy the Aether Kindred who killed him. To that purpose, she has undertaken a vow of silence. She will not speak until all cosmic horrors are defeated. Her faithful abhor aberrations and are enemies of corruption in all its guises. Solyma's followers often foil the schemes of {@book Gorodyn|GrimHollowPG24|9|Gorodyn}, the Arch Daemon of Avarice.
While she is known as the Great Judge, Solyma has gained another epitaph from her worshippers in Ostoya: Lady Vengeance. The bloodiest family vendettas have been waged in her name.
Solyma is the patron of judges, honorable vigilantes, and hunters of eldritch abominations. Her paladins often devote themselves to vengeance and zeal.

entries.name: Morael
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Sacrifice}

Morael teaches that the truest virtue is sacrifice. To put others' needs before oneself is the greatest pursuit. He represents hope for a better tomorrow that comes by making sacrifices, both great and small. He offers strength to those who must surrender themselves for a great cause and is the patron of martyrs and heroes who seek no glory.
Morael's former mistress, Vetara, was the Goddess of Love. He continues her legacy of compassion and selfless devotion. He is opposed by {@book Malikir|GrimHollowPG24|9|Malikir} the Proud.
Paladins in service to Morael tend to give up their personal wrath. Characters who regret a history of transgressions may also seek redemption through sacrifice, looking to Morael's example.

entries.name: Miklas
entries: {@i Arch Seraph of Mercy}

Miklas does not have the power of her former deity Aurelia, Goddess of Protection; she can only offer aid to those already sick, wounded, or in distress. Her domain is healing and salvation—yet she cannot end suffering entirely.

It is believed that this new dark age offers no rest for Miklas the Merciful. The sick, wounded, impoverished, and oppressed all cry her name during their despair. She is opposed by {@book Tormach|GrimHollowPG24|9|Tormach}, the new Lord of War.
Miklas's clerics most often preach the sanctity of lie and purity. Doctors and apothecaries may also pray for the Arch Seraph's guidance in healing the sick and mending the injured.
Playing as a Cleric or Paladin
The gods' presence has waned. Divine power is rare in Etharis. As a Cleric or a Paladin, you are a rarity, and your patron calls upon you to project their power into this world.

Remember that Etharis has entered into a dark age, the likes of which has never been experienced. Its people are starved for guidance and want to believe in a power greater than themselves. As a wielder of divine magic, you may use that hunger to bring hope and light to the world, or you could twist it to your advantage. Rarity is a form of power, after all.

entries.name: Beleth
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Fear}

The Arch Daemon of Fear goes by other names: the Trickster, the Laughing Man, and Blackjester. Beleth's goal is to spread terror throughout the world, deriving gleeful pleasure from torturing mortals with their worst fears. He's the patron of tyrants and oppressors everywhere, but Beleth's cruel practical joke is that every one of these people end up fearing the very population they oppress. To him, that mortals often cause their own horrific downfall is atrociously funny.
Beleth has no fear of {@book Empyreus|GrimHollowPG24|9|Empyreus}, and any mention of the Arch Seraph causes Blackjester to smirk, as if from a private joke.

Despite his malintent, the Trickster has many worshippers. Bards and charlatans may take inspiration from Beleth's antics, inflicting fear on those deemed to deserve it. Warriors and priests who wield terror as a weapon also make Blackjester smile, whether they intend to or not.

entries.name: Venin
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Deceit}

Known as the Great Deceiver, Dusktongue, and the Hooded One, Venin is the master of illusion, lies, and insanity. She relishes the lies people tell themselves to justify depravity and cruelty. Venin's followers gain great skill in illusions and convincing others of their versions of the truth, but eventually even they lose track of what is truth and fiction.

Venin hates {@book Zabriel|GrimHollowPG24|9|Zabriel} and rewards those who assassinate the Arch Seraph's followers. Rogues, assassins, and other shadowy warriors may whisper prayers to Venin so that her darkness may conceal their deeds.

entries.name: Sitri
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Hedonism}

Sitri. His name slips from the tongue like honey, or blood. He tempts mortals with their basest worldly desires—gluttony, lust, and greed. It's said that Sitri has never forced a single soul to do evil but only presented the choice. Those who succumb eventually lose themselves to their addictions.
Sitri's most prized possessions are the souls of Aphaeleon's followers who fall to their desires. Sitri enjoys taunting the Arch Seraph with these mortals the most.

entries.name: Malikir
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Pride}

Malikir prefers the title "God." As the most powerful of the Arch Daemons, her domain is tyranny and the abuse of authority. She is known to corrupt heroes who start out as goodhearted but eventually give in to pride. Inevitably, her followers meet a terrible fate. Malikir considers her counterpart, {@book Morael|GrimHollowPG24|9|Morael}, as a weakling and not even worth mentioning.

Ambitious nobles, fame-seeking bards, and paladins who crave glory are among those most likely to believe themselves worthy of Malikir's blessings. Any warlock willing to make a pact with a fiend might also aspire to have Malikir as their patron.

entries.name: Tormach
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Wrath}

Lieutenant of the dead god Maligant, brother to the Arch Seraph {@book Empyreus|GrimHollowPG24|9|Empyreus}, known by such names as Manslayer and Marauder—Tormach has seized the domain of war. He is a patron and source of strength for many warriors.
