Among the armies of Unterland, there is a growing faith to honor Tormach as an Arch Seraph, though this is blasphemy in the eyes of the Hearthkeepers.

Unfortunately, he cannot offer strategy or wisdom, only mindless butchery and violence. For Tormach, winning comes second to dealing as much carnage and death as possible. Every battlefield is an altar to the Arch Daemon of Wrath, though his tributes are stolen time and again by the healing light of {@book Miklas|GrimHollowPG24|9|Miklas} the Redeemer. This fact infuriates him to no end.
Paladins who draw strength from Tormach take oaths of vengeance, glory, and slaughter. Barbarians filled with zealous rage might also kill in Tormach's name.

entries.name: Gorodyn
entries: {@i Arch Daemon of Avarice}

The Arch Daemon of Avarice is often called the King of Red Coins. He's a corrupted follower of Jezra, God of Commerce. In his boundless treasury, he counts the souls of those who have given themselves to hoarding wealth by whatever means necessary, leaving hunger and poverty for the rest of the world.
Gorodyn fears {@book Solyma|GrimHollowPG24|9|Solyma}, as he owns so much there is very little he can do to defend it all. He has taken to bribing mortals and other beings to fight her in his stead.
Daemon, Seraph, honestly, it's rather trite.
They're all after the same thing.
We're either useful to them or in their way.

entries.name: The Aether Kindred
entries: After the Aetheric War, the Kindred seemed to disappear. It's believed these horrors slept deeply after destroying so many of the gods, possibly requiring rest to heal from their wounds. Or perhaps they slumbered in the aftermath of much devouring.

The presence of the Kindred has returned to Etharis in more recent times. Abominations spawn from their dreams, and it's feared they may one day wake to take revenge on the rest of the gods' creations.

Spellcasters who wield either arcane or divine magic may draw their power from the Aether Kindred. One horrifying theory suggests that Numena is an Aether Kindred, and thus why arcane magic is so dangerous. Whether the case or not, warlocks who make pacts with ancient gods and sorcerers whose minds are wracked with aberrant visions are undeniably touched by the Aether Kindred.

Worship of the Kindred is exceedingly rare, but not unheard of in Etharis. They have no formal church or doctrine. Indeed, few people even dare learn their names except the eldritch clerics who lead their cults. Their desires are truly unknowable. Their existence is an existential horror.

entries.name: Vraigoroth
entries: {@i Kindred of Devouring}
A strange entity resembling an enormous maw ringed with fangs that sucks all light and energy into itself. Vraigoroth's Kindred are amorphous horrors that dissolve and amalgamate organic matter.
Worshippers of Vraigoroth hysterically claim that it shall eventually devour and absorb all life, though it's difficult to discern whether they're terrified or jubilant at such a notion.

entries.name: M'rorcameleth
entries: {@i Kindred of Dreams}

For some, the dream world is a nightmarish landscape made of flesh, punctuated by bulbous eyes and wriggling, worm-like trees. It's unclear if M'rorcameleth is the master of this realm or is in fact the realm itself.

Dreaming of M'rorcameleth is believed to be an omen of some impending doom. Each night M'rorcameleth tortures those who visit its dreamscape with premonitions of possible futures—often ones they are powerless to change.

entries.name: Pharazorthok
entries: {@i Kindred of Knowing}

A being that resembles an enormous tentacled tree with countless eyes upon its branches, Pharazorthok twists and grows beneath the surface of reality like roots through the soil. Where those roots pierce the veil, horrifying aberrations of eyes and teeth spawn.

It's whispered that Pharazorthok sees and knows all. Its branches can grasp like tendrils into mortal minds to peer into the Material Plane, manifesting horrific visions and sorcerous power in those it touches.

entries.name: Primal Magic
entries: Somewhere between arcane and divine magic is the magic of nature, called primal magic by those who differentiate between the three types. This force represents both the power of the elements and the power of things that grow, live, and die outside of the purview of either god or mortal.

Two main cultures in Etharis practice primal magic. The elves of Tol Leyemil believe the Spirits of Nature teach them how to cast spells by communing with the natural forces of the forests, mountains, and rivers. The {@book Valikan|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans} clans evoke their magic from the Primordials living within the Elemental Planes.

To divine spellcasters, the Primordials and Spirits of Nature are no different to the daemons and horrors from where arcanists draw their magic. Yet there is a deep spirituality to primal magic that is missing from the cold academia of arcane magic.

entries.name: The Primordic Cycle
entries: Earth. Fire. Ice. Air. These are the primordial elements that roiled and thundered at the beginning of time. The first living thing sparked into existence amongst this primal chaos, and the elements were all it consumed to survive. It shattered rocks with its teeth, gulped lava down its gullet, and devoured storms of wind and hail. The creature grew and grew, until its size encircled all that was. Every element tempested inside of it. This creature was called {@book Gormadraug|GrimHollowPG24|8|Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes}, the Great Prismatic Wyrm.

For long millennia, Gormadraug was the conscious world. Then one day a mountain called {@book Citrolach|GrimHollowPG24|9|Citrolach} awoke on the Wyrm's back. Citrolach's heavy footsteps split the earth to create valleys, and here the ice seeped to feed the growing rainforests. From these trees emerged the first tribes of mortals. These tiny creatures were like gnats upon Gormadraug's back, and the Prismatic Wyrm hated them for how they itched. Gormadraug lashed Citrolach for having wrought them. Thus began Citrolach's hatred of the Wyrm.

Morakesh exploded from Gormadraug's breath like lava and fire spewing forth from a volcano. The {@book Lady Morakesh|GrimHollowPG24|9|Lady Morakesh} was wielded like a weapon, forced to battle to prove the extent of her fiery destructiveness. When the gods came to challenge Gormadraug's strength, Morakesh burned them to husks—composing a symphony of agonized screams for the Wyrm's sick pleasure.

Gormadraug tested even his own strength against Morakesh, extinguishing her flames to prove she was no match for him. The Lady harbored a burning hatred. The cinders of betrayal flared inside her heart.

The fires of Morakesh melted the remaining ice that formed the Endless Sea, releasing {@book Alondo|GrimHollowPG24|9|Alondo}, Lord of the Depths. Wishing to be left in solitude, Alondo tried to escape Gormadraug deep beneath the seas. The Prismatic Wyrm followed to show all Primordials that nowhere was beyond his reach.

{@book Ilhara|GrimHollowPG24|9|Ilhara}, Queen of Air and Shadow, formed in the shadow and smoke of Morakesh's flames. Silent and unseen, she travelled throughout the realm learning of the discontent for Gormadraug's tyrannical rule. Ambitious to replace him, Ilhara found a hero among mortals to kill Gormadraug.
Contradicting Mythology
There are many different faiths and religions in Etharis. The details between them can contradict each other, as is the nature of scriptures and ancient stories.

In some versions of the Primordic Cycle, {@book Gormadraug|GrimHollowPG24|8|Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes} clashed with the ancient gods of Etharis before the Aetheric War. The gods tried chaining the Prismatic Wyrm at first, but his size made him unable to be restrained. Gormadraug sired the four lesser Primordials as weapons against the gods. All were defeated before being cast from the heavens.

In the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire}, it's explained that Gormadraug was created by the gods, though his appearance is rare in sermon or scripture. Valikan stories claim the Prismatic Wyrm awoke first and that the gods followed later.
Which version of the story is told depends where in Nordenland or Grarjord one travels. Which version of the story is true, no mortal can ever know.

entries.name: The Kentigern Saga
entries: Enduring {@book Gormadraug|GrimHollowPG24|8|Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes}'s tyranny since time began, each of the Primordials ached to be free of their sire. They found their opportunity from an unexpected source: a man named Kentigern and his six companions.

{@book Ilhara|GrimHollowPG24|9|Ilhara} convinced her siblings to betray their sire and take the adventurers under their wing. Morakesh and {@book Citrolach|GrimHollowPG24|9|Citrolach} formed powerful magic weapons that could pierce Gormadraug's flesh. {@book Ilhara|GrimHollowPG24|9|Ilhara} composed for them a song that would put the Prismatic Wyrm to sleep. {@book Alondo|GrimHollowPG24|9|Alondo} taught them enchantments that would keep them alive, and he also told them where to strike Gormadraug's heart.

Thus, Kentigern and his companions slew Gormadraug and used his massive body to form the mortal realm. His flesh became the earth, and his spine the mountains that divide the center of Etharis. His blood became the frozen seas and therein his heart was thrown to form an island. Fragments of Gormadraug's scales can still be found today as precious stones. And thus the world was made.

Yet all four Primordials know that their sire was not truly destroyed, but merely slumbers in a state of hibernation. Even now, mortals obtain command over the elements from the Prismatic Wyrm. The day may come when Gormadraug awakens to wreak his vengeance across Etharis. The Primordials and mortals continually seek to prevent this from happening. Yet with the appearance of coldfire, it seems the Prismatic Wyrm's resurrection is coming ever closer.

entries.name: The Primordials
entries: Each of {@book Gormadraug|GrimHollowPG24|8|Gormadraug and the Seven Heroes}'s children rule over their own primordial realms. The Valikans believe Etharis exists in the gulf between the four Elemental Planes, where air, water, earth, and fire converge and clash together. It's said to be possible to enter the Elemental Planes where each element is strongest, and that druids draw their power from these realms like a wizard draws numen.

entries.name: Lady Morakesh
entries: {@i Primordial of Fire}

The Burning Lady, Red Queen, and Emberheart are among her many names. The Primordial of Fire holds court in her realm of Flamebourne. She commands azers, efreet, and fire giants in her army. Morakesh suffered the worst under Gormadraug, as the Wyrm saw her as his most destructive weapon.

As such she is highly capricious and easy to anger; she rewards characters with great riches as easily as she can turn them to cinders. Great care must be taken in petitioning the Red Queen. Blood and fire druids are among her worshipers, the most ardent of whom burn sentient beings alive as sacrifice.

entries.name: Alondo
entries: {@i Primordial of Ice and Water}

Lord of the Deep and ruler of the Endless Sea, Alondo controls the denizens of lakes, seas, and oceans. He is worshipped by the laneshi. Aside from Kentigern, Alondo has no love for mortals and wishes to be left alone. That said, he is the most conscientious of his ilk, working diligently to prevent the return of Gormadraug.

Still, rumors persist that he has something to do with the outbreak of coldfire plaguing Etharis. Blood druids who would invoke Alondo must first slay one of his hated enemies and hurl its heart into the sea.

entries.name: Citrolach
entries: {@i Primordial of Earth and Stone}

The Living Mountain masters over all those who pledge themselves to earth and stone. He is the progenitor of stoneborn trolls and earth elementals, who emerge from his underground realm of Stonereach. At times through history Citrolach has been worshipped by dwarves and gnomes who delve for riches beneath the earth. He is also important to druids of the land and moon, who believe his power extends to plants and the denizens of the forests.

To summon Citrolach, one must surrender a fortune in gems and gold, all of which are returned by the Primordial to the ground from where it was taken. Take care, however. Notoriously stubborn, Citrolach has held to a particular stand for centuries. The saying goes that it is easier to move a mountain than convince Citrolach to change his mind.

entries.name: Ilhara
entries: {@i Primordial of Air and Shadow}

The Queen of Air and Shadow is the most ambitious Primordial of all. Her spies hide in the smoke of candles and lurk in empty shadows to find secrets from among mortals and spirits alike. Her ultimate aim is to be powerful enough to fill the void left by Gormadraug. Ilhara requires that a dark secret be whispered into the air before she can be summoned.
Rangers who stalk the gloom or deal in poisons may petition Ilhara for aid. She is of particular importance to the spies of Clan Morgöng in Valika.

entries.name: Gormadraug
entries: {@i Primordial of All Elements}

The Great Prismatic Wyrm is also a powerful source of primal magic, though he doesn't know this in his current state. There is no known way to invoke him. Moreover, worship of the Wyrm is forbidden throughout the {@book Valikan Clans|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans}. The Primordials also warn that drawing power from Gormadraug may even awaken him, and they readily smite any druids or rangers who try.

entries.name: The Faerie Courts
entries: Before the Era of Expansion, the Faerie Courts were worshipped like deities in Caer Neiada—the realm now called the {@book Charneault Kingdom|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Charneault Kingdom}. The elves of that region believed they were descendants of the Archfey and that magic was only possible by listening to the Spirits of Nature. In ancient times it's said many more elves were capable of magic than today.

Similar beliefs are found in eastern Nordenland and parts of Valika. Though these more human cultures feel less directly connected to the Faerie Courts, even regarding the Archfey like capricious devils, they still train mages and make pacts to wield primal magic.

name: Chapter 10: Factions of Etharis
entries: The major factions of Etharis have some striking similarities, regardless of their motivations and goals. They have endured through trials, or in response to threats, and overtly or covertly permeate every stratum of society, whether they hold sway in one nation, multiple nations, or throughout all of Etharis.

Another element that transcends all of the factions discussed here—and so many of the lesser factions in Etharis—is simple to understand: power. Sometimes this power comes from the magical ability of its members. Other times it comes from the knowledge and influence that the group's leaders and members can bring to bear. The power may even come from the fear that the faction inspires in those it controls. Regardless, it's this power that makes Etharis's factions so formidable.

With power, of course, comes the will to use that power. Many times, this power works on behalf of the characters, and for society as a whole. The characters may have a symbiotic relationship with these factions: the factions can offer the characters a small piece of that power, as well as a sense of direction and goals to achieve.

On the other hand, each of these factions may abuse that power, making them institutions that the characters must oppose. Just as the characters in a Grim Hollow campaign must constantly be on guard against their own corruption as they gain power, these factions have the capability to perform and propagate great evil in the name of doing good.

entries.name: The Arcanist Inquisition
entries: {@i "Arcane magic is a scourge on all of Etharis, and no mortal can be trusted to practice it without becoming corrupted."}

When Montego Valieda unified what would become the {@book Castinellan Provinces|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces} into a single nation, one of his first acts was the creation of a religious order that would hunt down arcanists and prevent them from using their powers to threaten the newly formed nation. This order became known as the Arcanist Inquisition. Thereafter, Montego was referred to with reverence as Sagrado.

Valieda claimed that the Arch Seraph Empyreus spoke to him, declaring that arcane magic was a curse that endangered its wielders and everyone who associated with them. With the Inquisition embracing this warning as a core tenet, efforts began to control and limit the use of magic in Castinella.

The Inquisitors were tasked with patrolling the provinces, spreading their teachings about the dangers of magic, and stirring mistrust of arcanists among the population. After some time, they began arresting those known to be magically gifted and bringing them to segregated cells, where they would live a life of humble service to the Arch Seraph. The Inquisitors were eager to find these people at the youngest possible age, as they claimed the threat of magic could be more easily contained in the young, whose magical abilities had not fully emerged.

After completing his Seraph-mandated tasks, Sagrado Valieda returned to the land known as the Shattered Peaks to bring his message to the dragonborn. As missionaries of the Arch Seraph Empyreus spread their teachings across Etharis, some dragonborn migrated back to the Castinellan Provinces. Centuries of wandering and resentment at the absence of their ancient gods left the scattered dragonborn people ready for a new cause.

The dragonborn showed little hesitation to wield the light Empyreus offered. Seeing this, Sagrado Valieda seized the opportunity to strengthen Castinella and Empyreus's theocracy even more. Valieda announced that all dragonborn were welcome to reconstruct their former city of Ember Cairn, which had fallen eons ago, as long as they were raising it to the glory of Empyreus. As the dragonborn were now the most zealous followers of the Divine Seraph, Valieda made Ember Cairn the headquarters of his newly created Arcanist Inquisition and raised the Cathedral of Embers.

One of the three huge rock formations within Ember Cairn has been excavated to house the headquarters of the Arcanist Inquisition. This vast underground complex contains many prison cells and interrogation rooms for people accused of having magical abilities or committing violations of the sacred laws. Currently, the complex is ruled by the Lord Inquisitor Crevax, a seven-foot-tall dragonborn whose devotion to the sacred teachings of Empyreus is only outmatched by Sagrado Valieda himself.

The Arcanist Inquisition Headquarters is a maze through which only well-trained Inquisitors can find their way. According to rumors, the cells have no locks, and the Inquisitors let prisoners loose to determine whether or not they have magical abilities: if they find an exit, they are immediately recaptured and found guilty of magical impurity. Whether true or not, the fact remains that very few people are released from this place. Those who do emerge seldom escape with a healthy body and mind.

Characters may find that their goals and the Arcanist Inquisition's coincide, making the faction a popular one in the eyes of adventurers looking to earn much coin and fight so-called seditious evil. The strict doctrines, moral purity, and unexamined creed of the Inquisition are attractive to those who like their moral compasses unwavering in the face of scrutiny...or truth.

That strict and resolute dogma, however, almost always fails to satisfy when the messy truth of the people of Etharis reveals itself. Not all arcanists are evil, of course. Some innocent lives are lost when overzealous Inquisitors let their judgment be swayed by ambition, fear, or corruption. Characters working for the Arcanist Inquisition find themselves having to make hard moral choices—any hesitation to enforce the Inquisition's harsh divine laws is often seen as an unforgivable sin.

entries.name: The Augustine Trading Company
entries: {@i "Success in all ventures and circumstances requires the unhindered flow of goods and services."}

Amid the splendors of {@book Morencia|GrimHollowPG24|8|The City of Morencia} sits an organisation that guards its treasure as jealously as a dragon defends its hoard. The Augustine Trading Company is an ancient and venerable institution that maintains a presence in every country, from the southern reaches of {@book Castinella|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces} to the misty city of Castalore.

The company was named after its founder, Manoch Augustus, a clergyman who left the priesthood amid a scandal involving the church's finances. With few options left, he turned his talents to commerce. After a number of false starts, Augustus found his way into a lucrative profession: the weapons trade.

With the help of some investors from within the nobility, he founded an arms trading company in Morencia. With the gold he earned, he purchased land and lent coin to the rich. After systematizing predatory lending practices and enforcing them ruthlessly, the Augustine Trading Company ended up as the wealthiest faction in all of Etharis. They are one of the few non-governmental entities rich enough to hire the services of the Company of Free Swords. However, through the machination of its latest head, Kohlys "Blazing Brain," the line between company and government has blurred as an arm of the Augustine Trading Company became a dominant force on the Morencian Supreme Council.

The company maintains trade routes that run all the way to Grarjord and beyond. Each shipment is guarded by a contingent of professional soldiers, wizards, and priests. Even the raiders of Tyburn think twice before attacking these floating fortresses. The company currently has a hand in every industry, including food production, construction, labor, crafts, and education. It oversees one of the finest wizard colleges in Morencia. It has even lured legendary smiths from Rauland into its stable of weaponsmiths.

It is also no secret that the company has moved into a new era of weapon trading: breeding monsters to serve on the battlefield. Their first successful venture involved lycanthropic thralls they call "Warbreed." Rumors abound that the company is currently experimenting with eldritch mutants. While this current venture is frowned upon by the clergy, certain governmental representatives have embraced such forward-thinking ideas with open arms.

Local branch leaders don't like to risk the lives of their specially trained forces on foolish missions: guarding the lives of the leaders and the safety of shipments is a more valuable and wiser use of resources. The Trading Company would rather use expendable mercenaries than send representatives into dangerous situations.

For such adventurers, the pay is generous compared to the prospects of delving into monster lairs for loose coins—being selected to perform a mission for the Augustine Trading Company is considered a triumph for any adventuring party. The tasks are usually quite clear and uncomplicated. That is, as long as the party has few morals and even fewer scruples.

The Trading Company puts profit above people, meaning that missions often end with the adventuring party having to decide if they are going to do the proper thing or the expedient, profitable thing. Those who choose the latter are rewarded. Those who chose the former find themselves named enemies of the Augustine hierarchy, and that is a dangerous place to be.

However, even those who faithfully serve the Trading Company often become liabilities if they learn a dangerous truth. Someone who takes coin to do evil and keep secrets is just as likely to reveal those secrets for the right price. Even the most obsequious mercenaries working for the Trading Company find that after years of service, a mission is presented that they are not meant to return from.

entries.name: The Company of Free Swords
entries: {@i "Blood and Honor, Strength in Steel"}

If one needs a war won, a powerful monster removed, or a dirty job completed, the Company of Free Swords is the top mercenary group to call—assuming you have the coin. But despite the name, the company's services are far from free.

An ancient and elite mercenary company, the Free Swords have tipped the scales of war for many nations and provinces. It has the distinction of (according to the rumors that it spreads about itself) toppling a frost giant, rooting out an Arch Daemon, and sending a servant of the Aether Kindred back into the void. The company includes warriors, wizards, priests, druids, rogues, and spies among its ranks. It has a branch in nearly every corner of the continent.

The Free Swords began over a century ago as a military unit in the rebel province of Falaris, an area of southeastern Bürach along the Grey Spine. Serving a tyrant king, conscripted soldiers fought countless battles against the Bürach army. After receiving orders to raze a village of hapless peasants who refused to join the rebellion, Commander Darion Harkin declared he'd had enough. His entire unit deserted and formed their own organization. When the rebel king tried to have Harkin killed, the commander turned the tables and assassinated him instead.

Harkin was forgiven by the Bürach commanders for taking part in the rebellion, laying the foundations for the Company of Free Swords. Harkin proclaimed his organization one that prioritizes the welfare of the soldier rather than the ruler. Harkin famously declared, "If we must deprive this world of parents and their kin, and be killed as we do so, let it be for the highest coin, that we may die content."

Despite their mercenary ethics, the Free Swords have maintained a code of honor since their founding: stay loyal to the company, be honest in your dealings, and take vengeance on those who betray you or your comrades. They live simple lives free of excess, spending most of their time honing their fighting skills. Disciplined, loyal, and fearless, the company is ready for any war, confident in their success.

The Free Swords have spread across Etharis, taking on jobs from all who can afford their price. In over a century, they've become the largest mercenary organization in the land. No king, merchant, or official would dare cross them or fail to pay the Free Swords. They know the Company of Free Swords will take its price, one way or another.

Although it maintains branches in most of the population centers of Etharis, the main Free Swords Headquarters is in the city of Voros, where it has the easiest access to its four most profitable national interests: Bürach, {@book Ostoya|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Ostoyan Empire}, {@book Castinella|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Castinellan Provinces}, and {@book Charneault|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Charneault Kingdom}. New recruits, regardless of their military training or service before joining, must spend at least a year training and learning the ways of their new master: the Free Swords.

The characters' interactions with the Free Swords may range from uncomfortable interactions while on competing missions to full membership in the organization. Members are allowed to leave the service of the Company of the Free Swords, but those leaving must do so with permission and while in good standing. The promise of discretion in its dealings with customers is one taken very seriously by the Free Sword leadership. Anyone risking the reputation of the Company does so at their own peril.

While the Free Swords do not embrace outright evil, immoral, or unethical acts, the glint of gold can blur the line between good and evil. Some commanders might refuse truly heinous jobs, while others may sign off on slaughtering the innocent for personal gain.

It's not difficult to see how adventuring parties could act as a special force within the Free Swords, while others could be avowed enemies of the group when atrocities are committed by agents of the Company.

entries.name: The Crimson Court
entries: {@i "I have slept since the world was young. Yet now that I'm awake, all I feel is hunger."}

The sun-starved nation of Ostoya is ruled by a cabal of vampire nobles called the Crimson Court. This loose system of nobility and feudal servants oversees every aspect of the nation, from the highest noble decrees to the lowest elements of criminality. The power of the Crimson Court relies on the fear it instills in its population or the bribes it offers to those who do not fear it.

Though seven noble members sit atop the Crimson Court, ruling side by side, they are far from allies. None of them achieved a high seat on the court by being kind sovereigns, and none of them committed the atrocious, dangerous acts needed to attain their position in expectation of serving another leader.

The vampire aristocracy vie for the position of Grand Duke, the leader of the Crimson Court. The schemes the courtiers employ to attain that status span decades and often take precedence over their governmental responsibilities. With rampant scheming and treachery, it is not unusual for the Court's leadership to change frequently and unexpectedly.

Each of the lords of the Crimson Court are responsible for one of the provinces, including the rebellious province of Raevo. Failure to maintain obedience and control over a province can result in a member of the Crimson Court being deposed or even eliminated—which might tempt an ambitious rival to foment such trouble.

All members of the Crimson Court's leadership are immensely powerful. The lower nobility jockey for their own forms of power, constantly seeking to gain the influence necessary to move up. Assassinations, power plays, cunning deceits, and social engineering are all weapons the vampires employ to wound each other without undermining their collective strength.

As the ruling body of a nation, the Crimson Court has a great deal of power, but also a great deal of responsibility. Adventuring parties are a favorite tool for the Crimson Court to use in pursuit of their goals. Mundane tasks like dealing with marauding monsters in their province that are beyond the capabilities of local guards or militia are prime jobs for adventurers. Mercenaries might also be hired to deal with local rebellions or unrest, allowing the vampire nobles to stay at a distance from the trifling affairs of mere mortals.

More illustrious, but also more deadly, are characters being used as pawns in internal Crimson Court machinations, as one noble attempts to gain prestige or power at the expense of another. Often characters think they are eliminating an evil threat for the greater good, only to realize when it is too late that they simply made it possible for a greater evil to thrive.

entries.name: The Ebon Syndicate
entries: {@i "The night is our kingdom."}
It is said that beneath every unexplained success lies an undiscovered crime. If so, the successes behind the Ebon Syndicate are innumerable. And so are the crimes.

In many respects, the Ebon Syndicate is the Augustine Trading Company's dark mirror—an organization with a finger in every major criminal trade. Assassination, kidnapping, drug trafficking, theft, and the black market all fall within their purview.
The syndicate employs and controls thugs, beggars, thieves, professional killers, extortionists, and spies. Its Shadow Academy ensures these agents operate with the highest skill and efficiency.

The Ebon Syndicate began with an Altenheim-based bandit named Hiram Greyman. One night, after a battle with the city watch left his men dead and Hiram badly wounded, the bandit fled into a hedge maze. There, delirious from blood loss, he followed a hawk moth that led him to safety. Since then, Greyman thought himself favored by the gods and used the hawk moth as his personal symbol. He rebuilt his gang and went on to establish the Syndicate, which over the decades grew into the powerful shadow organization it is today.

After Greyman died, his name became a title passed on from one syndicate leader to the next. The current Greyman is Natalia Koscheii, who is infamous for her ability to escape the most meticulous assassination attempts.

The Syndicate's presence is felt in the largest and most prosperous cities of Etharis. In these areas, smaller criminal organizations bow to the Syndicate. Freelancers receive one warning about plying their trades in Syndicate territory before their lives are shortened, and any group that tries to muscle into the Syndicate's business is treated without mercy.

The Syndicate maintains a tenuous peace with the other factions. Groups like the Watchers and the Augustine Trading Company are technically at war with them, but often hire their services, as the Syndicate's spy network outmatches any other.

The Syndicate's insignia represents the hawk moth, a nocturnal creature known for its swift flight and short lifespan. Similarly, the agents of the Syndicate move quickly through darkness, taking what they want, with the full knowledge that life is short among fellow rogues.

The leadership of the Ebon Syndicate is managed through a system of local, regional, and global networks. Local leaders are trusted to handle day-to-day operations. They report to regional lieutenants who coordinate efforts and make sure that no local groups step out of line or come into conflict.

Higher-level operatives of the Syndicate, as well as regional and global leaders, tend to move frequently to avoid assassination attempts or law-enforcement efforts. Natalia Koscheii moves from city to city constantly, keeping herself safe and the leadership on its toes.

The Ebon Syndicate prefers to keep its actions and schemes in the shadows, especially in areas where they may not have the strongest foothold. In these cases, the Syndicate tends to hire mercenaries and freelance charlatans to do the most dangerous work. In this manner, characters who take commission work may never realize that the Ebon Syndicate was their true employer.

The opposite is also true. The Ebon Syndicate commits so many crimes that it is almost impossible for an investigation not to bring the investigators into contact, and invariably into conflict, with the Syndicate and their machinations.

entries.name: Monster Hunter Guilds
entries: {@i "Hire the knight if you want him to die bravely. Hire me if you want the job done."}

For as long as people have walked Etharis, there have been monsters. Mercurial fey drift through the woods, leading folk into danger whilst chuckling at their misfortunes. Monstrosities prowl the shadows, waiting to make a meal of the weak or unwary. In Etharis, even the dead are restless for blood. But the greatest threat facing commoners isn't any single beast or slumbering catastrophe—it's ignorance and superstition.

The profession of hunting and killing monsters arose as an inevitable necessity. Anyone can wield a sword, but it takes particular expertise to locate a kikimora hiding in a home or know that stoneborn trolls aren't weak to fire. Monster hunting is an ancient trade. What separated hunters from common warriors was curiosity—a genuine desire to understand one's enemy. Knowledge rose as the monster hunter's most lethal weapon.

Recent times have seen an increase in organized hunting guilds. Killing monsters has become a corporatized trade due to hunters banding together and sharing knowledge, resources, and contracts. Some guilds have arranged themselves into a formalized structure like a mercenary company, with captains accepting quests and assigning them to teams of hunters. Such guilds are likely to only take larger contracts that require their combined skills and cover their increased costs. Other guilds are a looser associations of lone wanderers who gather once a year in a dusty guild hall to share news and stories from the road. Hunters in these guilds may choose to travel in pairs: a mentor and apprentice. They protect each other, pass down their teachings, and continue guild traditions.

It's difficult for any individual hunting guild to grow to the size or reputation of a mercenary band like the Company of Free Swords. Difficulty recruiting new hunters has been a constant issue for the guilds. Folks are braver when their foe is also mortal, rather than facing a shadow daemon or a nest of infernal spiders. Most guilds also won't accept contracts on commoners, and killing monsters will never be as big an industry as killing people.

What also keeps the guilds separated is their varied, and sometimes incompatible, methods of monster hunting. Some guilds insist on fighting with sword, shield, and spear—teaching their pupils precisely where to strike to slay their foe. Others employ more esoteric methods, such as learning spells or employing sophisticated traps. Then there are the guilds who embrace fully eldritch methods by devouring monster flesh to self-inflict physical mutations, horrifying many other hunters. Public attitudes towards the hunting guilds also vary due to their methods. Some folk value hunters as professionals required to keep civilization safe. Others abhor them as extortionists and freaks, who likely consort with the monsters they claim are their enemies.

Monster hunters are likely to join larger adventuring parties. A guild hunter in a party might continue to receive news, contracts, and even instructions from their guild whilst adventuring. A hunting guild might also hire the expertise of an adventuring party directly, consulting a wizard in matters of the arcane, or asking a ranger to guide them through a dense wilderness.

Conversely, adventuring parties may clash with guild hunters when competing for the same bounty or if the party is working for a preternatural employer such as the Crimson Court. The characters may be targeted by a hunting guild themselves if one among their number is monstrous, suffering from a transformation or raised with an eldritch heritage. While most guild hunters would balk at accepting contracts on people, some who are less scrupulous or more desperate might take payment to cut down anyone who blurs the line between mortal and monster.

entries.name: Morbus Doctore
entries: {@i "No knowledge should be feared, because knowledge is power."}

The Morbus Doctore appeared shortly after the release of the Weeping Pox, although some swear the members of the medical and scientific organization were walking the streets before the first cases of the disease presented themselves. Regardless of when the organization was founded, the members are easy to spot: boots and gloves dried with blood, strange coats and satchels hung with vials, and frightful masks meant to protect them from the disease. They now wander the {@book city of Liesech|GrimHollowPG24|8|The City of Liesech}—as well as other major metropolitan areas of Etharis—looking for newly infected people to treat.

The combined medical and scientific knowledge held by the members of the Morbus Doctore is staggering. While examining, treating, studying, and caring for those afflicted with the Weeping Pox, the physicians have taken advantage of the rare opportunity to examine the living and the dead at such close proximity.

For the poor and infirm, the Morbus Doctore is both a blessing and a curse. A doctor coming into your life likely means you or someone close to you is suffering from a deadly disease. However, with the doctor also comes relief from the pain such diseases bring. And then there's always the rare but rumored possibility of a full recovery.

However, no medical breakthrough is achieved without suffering. Some who've seen the Morbus Doctore at work claim that they revel in the death they see every day. They torture their "patients." They perform cruel and nauseating experiments with no regard for their charges. They graft skin, extract marrow, and inject tinctures. Then they observe with fascination, scribbling notes in the name of science. The most insidious rumor suggests they might even have been responsible for the spread of the Weeping Pox in the first place.

Members of the group share information with each other regularly, ensuring that no valuable data or knowledge is lost or hoarded. They do not share their research outside their cabal, and it isn't clear to which authority Morbus Doctore answers. If there is a leader or leadership hierarchy in the organization, that information is not publicly available.

While most of the Morbus Doctore hide their identities behind their plague masks, the group relies on less mysterious presentations when interacting with the public. Local doctors and nurses are called upon to interact with a skittish public. Maintaining strong relationship between citizens and their local doctors can foster trust with the Morbus Doctore. In return for this service, the local physicians get the benefit of the expertise, knowledge, and cures held by the Morbus Doctore.

Adventurers are another valuable tool for the Morbus Doctore. Whether it's extracting rare components from monsters, acting as security in dangerous situations, or tracking down delirious patients who escaped from quarantine, the physicians rely on experts and mercenaries in areas they don't thrive.

With so much death in the vicinity of the Morbus Doctore, accusations of evildoing are inevitable—and may be well founded. Whether the entire group is corrupt, or individual members tend to overstep their moral and ethical bounds, characters might be called upon to investigate the demise of an otherwise healthy patient. And if there is a larger conspiracy at the heart of the Morbus Doctore, adventurers will be at the center of that investigation.

entries.name: Order of Dawn
entries: {@i "The scourge of undeath will disintegrate before the cleansing light of the dawn."}

When the Crimson Court was established, a number of {@book Ostoyans|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Ostoyan Empire} fled the rule of their vampire overlords and settled in the sympathetic province of Raevo. These dissidents occupied a fort outside of Castalore, where they formed a militia fully committed to the destruction of the undead and the liberation of the rest of Ostoya. Thus the Order of Dawn was born.

From their inauguration into the Order, each member is taught to hate undeath. Every such monster has been studied and dissected, their weaknesses carefully catalogued. While few compared to national armies or other global organizations, each member of the Order is trained and battle-ready. They're prepared to give their lives for their cause, and no vampire can expect mercy from them.

Currently, the Order is led by General Artemios Dulka, an Ostoyan noble who abdicated his title to escape the vampire curse. In retaliation, the vampires massacred his family that remained in Nov Ostoya. A good man at heart, Dulka is driven by hatred of the Crimson Court aristocracy and will stop at nothing to destroy the undead.

While the group maintains strongholds on the fringes of Ostoya and in the rebel province of Raevo, they also operate safe houses and spy networks within the Crimson Court's territory of the Ostoyan provinces. To that end, the Order of Dawn recruits freelance spies and mercenaries from whose ranks they draw new members. The Order is suspicious by nature, especially of shapeshifters and glamoured thralls, and so only characters who prove themselves are permitted to join as full initiates.

The Order of Dawn, however, is rigid in its deeds and principles. They exhume graveyards to burn or desecrate the cadavers, ignoring the mournful wails of the local population. They're intolerant of dhampir, who they do not trust. They prosecute whoever aids the Crimson Court as a collaborator, even if the person in question was given little choice. In these situations, characters may find themselves at odds with the members of the Order of Dawn, especially if they've taken a contract from Ostoyan nobility or cannot abide the Order's strict doctrine.
The Order of Dawn may protect and feed the people who suffer under tyranny, but at what cost?

entries.name: The Prismatic Circle
entries: {@i "Only blood and sacrifice can keep the Prismatic Wyrm slumbering, sparing the world from its apocalyptic wrath."}

In the western island of Holgar, in the portion of the lands of {@book Valika|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans} known as Thrull, an order of druids known as the Prismatic Circle arose. Their tenets held that only war and bloodshed could keep the world safe from the reawakening of Gormadraug, the Prismatic Wyrm that birthed the Primordials. Several clans followed their teachings, turning Thrull into an area that made its primary mission, apart from survival, obedience to the Prismatic Circle.

In the east, another province arose in opposition to the Prismatic Circle: Kandar. The three clans that made up Kandar accepted newcomers from other nations and tried to dispel old superstitions about the Great Wyrm. They have advocated for peace and trade instead of the old Valikan ways of war and raiding. The two sides soon came to blows over their beliefs, and thus began the Thrull–Kandar War.

Hjadana, the Grand Druid of the Prismatic Circle, is spiritual leader of the Thrull people, and the true power behind the three clans that form Thrull. Hjadana truly believes that their way of living is saving the world from a great menace and an inevitable apocalyptic event. Thus, she exhorts each chief to continue the war against any who oppose them.

The Grand Druid, along with the other leaders of the Prismatic Circle, dwells within a hollow hill, surrounded by a fortress composed of living oak trees. Within this sacred area, she can command the air and earth itself to aid her in the sacred duties of her druids' circle. It is here where living sacrifices to the sleeping Gormadraug are made on holy days and hung from the oaken boughs.

Those opposing the Prismatic Circle's brutal domination of Thrull risk becoming sacrifices themselves. Hjadana preaches that the Coldfire Crisis plaguing eastern and central Valika is a sign that the Great Wyrm is stirring, and thus she commands all the clans to intensify their raids and sacrifices. Many believe her warnings, and she gains new converts by the day.

Characters are most likely to come into conflict with the Prismatic Circle within Valika, as the clans that support the Circle take and transport people to use as sacrifices. However, the raiders who sail south from Thrull to pillage towns and villages on the coastlines of other nations are a threat that adventurers may be hired to repel.

But what if the prophecy about the Prismatic Wyrm's awakening is true? Gormadraug is a central figure in the tales of all the {@book Valikan clans|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans}, and it is difficult to deny the increasing problems faced in the north. Some who don't accept the Prismatic Circle's violent ways still work with them, trying to understand and prevent the Coldfire Crisis from spreading.

entries.name: The Thaumaturge
entries: {@i "We who shape magic, shape the world."}
The Grand Thaumatic Order of archmages is a myth. Its existence has been a rumor throughout history, yet most commonfolk regard it as a fairy tale intended to frighten the conspiratorial.

The fanciful tale suggests a cabal of secretive archmages has shaped the course of history since the nations of Etharis began to take shape. They're responsible for many of the greatest calamities to befall the continent. Some claim they are the true puppeteers behind the Morbus Doctore and that they unleashed the Weeping Pox. Others suggest the Thaumaturge banished Ulmyr's Gate to eliminate their wizardly rivals.

There are more optimistic stories, as well. The truly downtrodden look to the Thaumaturge as a symbol of rebellion against authoritarian and intolerant regimes. They're believed to vex the Arcanist Inquisition—a hidden enemy the Inquisition both fears and denies the existence of. In Ostoya, the Lady of Masks is whispered to be a member. Therefore, resistance against the Crimson Court must be stoked by the Thaumaturge.

In the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire}, it's believed Martorius, whose magnificent castle spontaneously appeared atop the Rock-Teeth Mountains, is a member of the Thaumaturge. If such a thing could be true, his purpose remains a mystery. Why would he make his presence suddenly known? While it's whispered in taverns that tolerate such talk that Martorius has appeared in time to fight the Great Beast, the Hearthkeepers and nobility remain nervous. No agent sent to his castle without invitation has ever returned.

Fables about the Thaumaturge have spread across Etharis, and powerful mages in every nation are assumed to be connected. Yet the fact remains no evidence of the Thaumaturge's actual existence has ever been produced.

Occasionally, characters may receive a mysterious contract. An unknown benefactor has asked for an artifact to be recovered. A noble must be assassinated. A prisoner is to be freed. Yet there are many organizations in Etharis who prefer keeping their involvement in certain affairs secret. Following such threads has never led back to the Thaumaturge.

In other circumstances, characters may return to a village they swore to protect only to discover it has disappeared as if it never existed. Or perhaps, a military leader contracts a disease, unexpectedly. Within days he's dead, yet nobody else appears ill. Such impossible events are whispered to befall those who displease the Thaumaturge.
The Thaumaturge is considered a myth. But if they do exist, their greatest spell was convincing the world they don't.

entries.name: The Watchers of the Faithful
entries: {@i "Though the gods no longer grace the heavens, the Arch Seraphs still guide the way."}

After the loss of the gods, many of their clerics and paladins rallied to the remaining Arch Seraphs to hold the fabric of their society together. Throwing their faith behind the Seraphs, the major churches reformed into a large organization known as the Watchers of the Faithful. Composed of priests, paladins, chaplains, and other clergy, the Watchers have dedicated themselves to preserving the old faiths in this new form.

The Watchers are a comparatively young faction but are highly organized and zealous in the pursuit of their goals. They follow Sagrado Valieda's Eternal Dogma: revere the Arch Seraphs, follow their laws as handed down by the Divine Speaker, and destroy all that is unholy and evil in their sight—through any means necessary.

The Watchers are composed of six sects, each one following a particular Arch Seraph. Each elects a High Priest to rule the sect, with the most populous sect having the greatest weight in votes. The current High Priest, Nola Cirdanal, is a follower of Empyreus. Her uncompromising stance has put the Watchers on the path to war against several factions that have fallen afoul of the Eternal Dogma.

The Watchers have urged the Arcanist Inquisition to undertake various holy missions across Etharis in an effort to stamp out all that is, in Cirdanal's words, "corrupt, heretical, iniquitous, or displeasing to the eyes of Heaven." They have pursued the Augustine Trading Company for its efforts at weaponizing monsters, the {@book Ostoyan|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Ostoyan Empire} nobility for falling to vampirism, and the {@book Valikan|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Valikan Clans} tribes for raiding and worshipping the elements.

The Gods' End legitimized the Watchers' reach, and therefore the Inquisition's, into other nations, particularly the {@book Bürach Empire|GrimHollowPG24|8|The Bürach Empire}. To the shock of those in power, their presence has often found support among the citizens. Thousands flocked to the Watchers, with many going on pilgrimages to far-flung areas to spread the word of righteousness through diplomacy or warfare. Due to their growing support, money continues to flow into the coffers of the Grand Temple.
Watchers of the Faithful? More like watchers for any hint of humanity in a person.
